name: Review a Paper
description: Critical review of methodology, statistical rigor, limitations, and constructive feedback
prompt: Read the uploaded paper carefully, then write a structured, evidence-grounded critical review: (1) Summary of the central question, methods, and key findings in your own words; (2) Methodology assessment, including study design, sample size and power, controls, confounders, and whether the methods support the claims; (3) Statistical rigor, checking test choice, assumptions, multiple-comparison handling, effect sizes, and reproducibility; (4) Strengths; (5) Limitations, weaknesses, and any unsupported or overstated claims; (6) Constructive, actionable suggestions for improvement, prioritized by importance. Ground every critique in specific text, figures, or tables from the paper, citing section or figure numbers; never invent quotes or data. If a key detail (e.g., methods or stats) is missing or ambiguous and changes your assessment, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise note it as a gap. Save the review as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and give a brief summary in chat.

name: Write a Paper
description: Draft a full manuscript from uploaded data or results
prompt: Using only the uploaded data and results, draft a scientific manuscript on the topic of: {topic}. Follow IMRaD: Abstract (structured: background, methods, results, conclusion); Introduction (context, gap, objective, and hypothesis); Methods (enough detail for reproducibility); Results (findings in logical order, each with the exact statistics and a reference to its figure/table, no interpretation); Discussion (interpretation against prior work, mechanisms, limitations, future directions); and References. Report only results present in the uploaded material; never fabricate data, statistics, or citations, and flag with [CITATION NEEDED] or [VERIFY] any claim that requires a real reference or external source. Note key limitations and caveats. If an essential detail is missing or ambiguous and would materially change the draft, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool before proceeding; otherwise state your assumptions explicitly. Save the manuscript (with tables, figure captions, and references) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the draft briefly in chat.

name: Write a Review Article
description: Comprehensive literature synthesis with gap analysis and future directions
prompt: Write a comprehensive, narrative review article on: {topic}. Search the literature thoroughly using real, verifiable sources; synthesize findings across studies rather than summarizing them one by one; and organize the body into clear thematic sections with a short introduction (scope and significance) and conclusion. Identify points of consensus, open controversies and conflicting evidence, methodological trends, research gaps, and concrete future directions. Cite every claim to a real paper with author, year, and title; never fabricate references or overstate the strength of evidence, and flag with [VERIFY] anything you could not confirm. Note the limitations of the current literature and of your synthesis. If the scope is too broad or ambiguous to be useful, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state the scope you assumed. Save the review (with a reference list) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and give a brief summary in chat.

name: Edit / Rewrite Manuscript
description: Improve clarity, grammar, flow, and scientific tone of a manuscript
prompt: Improve the writing quality of the uploaded manuscript while strictly preserving its original meaning, claims, data, and technical accuracy. Fix grammar, spelling, and punctuation; sharpen sentence-level clarity and concision; strengthen transitions within and between sections; ensure a consistent, formal scientific tone and consistent terminology and tense; and tighten verbose or redundant passages. Do not add new claims, citations, or results, and do not alter numerical values or reported statistics. Where wording is genuinely ambiguous, preserve the author's apparent intent and flag the spot rather than guessing. Provide the full revised manuscript, and in chat give a brief summary of the main categories of changes you made plus any passages that need author attention. Save the revised manuscript as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download.

name: Write a Rebuttal
description: Point-by-point response to reviewer comments
prompt: Draft a professional, point-by-point rebuttal (response-to-reviewers) letter addressing every comment in the uploaded document. Open with a brief thank-you to the editor and reviewers. Then, for each comment, in order and clearly numbered: (1) restate the reviewer's concern verbatim or faithfully paraphrased; (2) give a respectful, substantive response that either agrees and describes the fix or politely justifies the original choice with evidence; (3) specify the exact change made and where (section, line, or figure), quoting revised text where helpful. Base every response only on the manuscript and provided materials; never invent new results or citations, and flag with [VERIFY] anything needing confirmation from the authors. Keep the tone courteous and confident, never defensive. If a reviewer comment is unclear or requires a decision only the authors can make, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise note the assumption. Save the letter as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize key responses in chat.

name: Format for Journal
description: Reformat manuscript to match a target journal's submission guidelines
prompt: Reformat the uploaded manuscript to comply with the submission guidelines of {journal}. First look up that journal's current author instructions, and state the version/date you relied on. Adjust section order and headings, abstract type and word limit, overall word/figure/table limits, reference and in-text citation style, figure and table formatting and numbering, title-page and author/affiliation conventions, and any other journal-specific requirements (e.g., highlights, structured abstract, reporting checklists). Do not change scientific content, data, or meaning; only restructure and reformat. Where the manuscript exceeds a limit or is missing a required element, flag it clearly rather than inventing content, and list any guideline points you could not verify. If a guideline is ambiguous in a way that materially affects formatting, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise note your assumption. Save the reformatted manuscript as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the changes and any outstanding compliance items in chat.

name: Write an Abstract
description: Draft or improve a structured abstract for a manuscript
prompt: Write a structured abstract for the uploaded manuscript, or from these details: {details}. Cover: (1) Background/objective (1-2 sentences); (2) Methods (2-3 sentences); (3) Results (2-3 sentences with the key quantitative findings and statistics); (4) Conclusions (1-2 sentences stating significance and implications). Keep it within {word_limit} words. Use only information present in the source material; never fabricate results, numbers, or citations, and flag with [VERIFY] anything you cannot confirm. Ensure the abstract is self-contained, conveys the single key message, avoids undefined abbreviations, and matches the manuscript's claims without overstating them. If the key findings or word limit are missing or ambiguous in a way that changes the abstract, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumption. Save the abstract as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and paste the final text into chat with the word count.

name: Write a Cover Letter
description: Draft a journal submission cover letter
prompt: Write a concise, professional cover letter (roughly one page) for submitting the uploaded manuscript to {journal}, addressed to the Editor-in-Chief. Include: (1) the manuscript title and a brief summary of the study; (2) the key findings and why they matter; (3) a specific, non-generic statement of why the journal and its readership are the right fit; (4) a statement of originality, that the work is not under concurrent consideration elsewhere, and that all authors approved the submission; (5) suggested (and, if relevant, opposed) reviewers. Draw all claims from the manuscript; never overstate findings or invent details, and flag with [VERIFY] anything you cannot confirm (e.g., author names, corresponding-author contact). If essential details are missing or ambiguous, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise note placeholders. Save the cover letter as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write a Methods Section
description: Write a detailed methods section from protocol or data
prompt: Based on the uploaded protocol, data, or experimental notes, write a detailed Methods section suitable for a journal manuscript, written in past tense and the conventional impersonal scientific style. Cover, with clear subheadings: (1) Study design and setting; (2) Materials, reagents, equipment, and where applicable catalog numbers, software, and versions; (3) Experimental procedures in enough operational detail for an independent lab to reproduce them; (4) Data collection methods and outcome measures; (5) Statistical analysis approach, including tests, assumptions, software, significance threshold, and handling of missing data. Use only details present in the uploaded material; never invent parameters, reagents, or values, and flag any gap with [DETAIL NEEDED] rather than guessing. Note ethics/approval and reporting-standard requirements where relevant. If a missing detail would materially affect reproducibility, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise list the gaps. Save the Methods section as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write a Results Section
description: Write a results section from data and figures
prompt: Write a Results section from the uploaded data, figures, and statistical outputs, reporting findings objectively in logical order with no interpretation, mechanism, or comparison to other work. For each result: (1) state the finding clearly in plain scientific prose; (2) reference the relevant figure or table by number; (3) report the exact statistics present in the source (test statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value, confidence interval, and effect size). {focus} Use only values that appear in the uploaded material; never fabricate or round away statistics or infer numbers that are not provided, and flag any missing or ambiguous value with [VERIFY] rather than guessing. Keep tense consistent and define abbreviations on first use. If a statistical output is unclear in a way that changes how a result is reported, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise note the gap. Save the Results section (with figure/table references) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write a Discussion Section
description: Write a discussion interpreting results in context of the literature
prompt: Write a Discussion section for the uploaded manuscript or these results: {findings}. Structure it as: (1) restate the main findings and their direct answer to the research question, without re-reporting every statistic; (2) interpret the findings in the context of existing literature, noting agreement and conflict with prior work; (3) discuss plausible mechanisms and the practical or theoretical implications; (4) candidly acknowledge limitations and their effect on interpretation; (5) suggest concrete future directions. Search the literature for real, verifiable sources to support every interpretive claim; never fabricate citations or overstate causation beyond what the data support, and flag with [VERIFY] anything you cannot confirm. If the findings provided are too sparse or ambiguous to interpret responsibly, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the Discussion section (with references) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write an Introduction
description: Write an introduction establishing context and rationale
prompt: Write an Introduction section for a paper on: {topic}, using the classic funnel structure: (1) broad context and significance of the field; (2) a focused review of key prior work, citing real, verifiable papers; (3) a clear statement of the gap or unanswered question; (4) the objective, hypothesis, and approach of the current study; (5) a brief signpost of what follows. Search the literature so that every citation is accurate (author, year, title); never fabricate references or attribute claims to the wrong source, and flag with [VERIFY] anything you cannot confirm. Build a logical argument that motivates the study rather than listing papers, and avoid overstating novelty. If the scope or intended contribution is ambiguous in a way that changes the framing, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the Introduction (with references) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Translate Manuscript
description: Translate a scientific manuscript between languages
prompt: Translate the uploaded scientific manuscript from {source_lang} to {target_lang}. Preserve all technical terminology using the conventional field-specific equivalents in the target language, maintain scientific precision and the original meaning, and keep all numbers, units, equations, citations, reference numbers, figure/table labels, and formatting intact and unchanged. Produce a translation that reads naturally to a native speaker rather than a literal word-for-word rendering, while never adding, omitting, or reinterpreting content. Flag any term, idiom, or passage where the translation is ambiguous or where a standard equivalent is uncertain, listing the alternatives rather than silently choosing. If a critical ambiguity would materially change the meaning, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise note your choice inline. Save the translated manuscript as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and give a brief summary in chat including any flagged terms.

name: Write Supplementary Materials
description: Prepare supplementary methods, figures, and tables
prompt: Prepare supplementary materials for the uploaded manuscript, derived strictly from its content and data. Include, as relevant: (1) detailed methods and parameters omitted from the main text for length; (2) supplementary figures, each with a self-contained caption; (3) supplementary tables with clear headers and units; (4) additional or sensitivity statistical analyses; (5) raw or summarized data tables. Number every item consistently (Fig. S1, Table S1, Supplementary Note 1, etc.), ensure each is referenced from the main text, and keep notation and units consistent with the main manuscript. Use only information present in the uploaded material; never fabricate data, figures, or statistics, and flag any gap with [DATA NEEDED] rather than inventing content. If a needed input is missing or ambiguous, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise note the gap. Save the supplementary document (with tables and figure captions) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the items prepared in chat.

name: Check & Fix References
description: Verify references, fix formatting, and find missing citations
prompt: Audit the references in the uploaded manuscript and produce {style}-formatted output. (1) Verify each cited work actually exists and is correctly attributed (authors, year, title, venue), using web/literature search, and flag any reference you cannot confirm rather than assuming it is valid; (2) fix formatting inconsistencies and bring both in-text citations and the reference list into a consistent style; (3) identify text claims that need a citation but lack one; (4) suggest more recent, authoritative, or directly relevant references where appropriate, clearly marked as suggestions; (5) note duplicate, retracted, or broken entries. Never invent citations, DOIs, or bibliographic details; mark anything unverified as [VERIFY]. If the citation style is unspecified or the manuscript mixes styles in a way that changes the fix, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state the style you applied. Save a report (corrected reference list plus a list of issues and suggested citations) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the key findings in chat.

name: Create a Graphical Abstract
description: Visual summary of key findings for a paper
prompt: Create a graphical abstract that summarizes the key findings of the uploaded paper or data in a single self-contained figure. First extract the actual central message, study system, and main result from the source; never invent data or results. Design a clear reading order (left-to-right or top-to-bottom) with 3-5 visual stages connected by arrows, generous whitespace, and minimal text labels (short phrases, not sentences). Use a colorblind-safe palette, consistent iconography and line weights, and legible fonts (around 14-18 pt equivalent) sized for a journal's typical ~12 x 6 cm abstract panel. Avoid chartjunk and misleading scales. If the key message, target journal, or audience is unclear and would change the design, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the abstract as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable SVG, and list the file names in chat.

name: Make a Publication Figure
description: Publication-quality plots and visualizations from data
prompt: Create publication-quality figures from the uploaded data using only real values present in the data. Inspect the data first, then choose the chart type that fits its structure and the comparison being made (e.g., bars for categorical counts, lines for trends, box/violin or scatter for distributions). Label every axis with units, add legends and clear tick formatting, and use a colorblind-safe palette with honest, non-truncated scales. Apply a clean, consistent style with legible fonts, minimal gridlines, and no chartjunk; show uncertainty (error bars, CI) where relevant. Target standard journal specs: vector-friendly layout, 300+ DPI, and single- or double-column proportions. If figure type, grouping, or target journal is ambiguous and would change the result, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Save each figure as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable source (SVG or PDF) and list the file names in chat. {instructions}

name: Build a Slide Deck
description: Scientific presentation slides for talks or lab meetings
prompt: Create a scientific slide deck (PPTX) from the uploaded paper or data for a {context}, tailoring depth, jargon, and pacing to that audience. Build: a title slide, a brief outline, background/motivation, a methods overview, key results with figures, discussion points, and conclusions, plus an acknowledgements/references slide. Use 16:9 widescreen, one core idea per slide, large legible fonts (titles ~32-40 pt, body ~20-24 pt), and concise bullets or visuals over dense text. Apply a consistent template, a colorblind-safe palette, high-contrast text, and reuse the paper's actual figures at high resolution with readable labels; never fabricate results. Add short speaker notes summarizing each slide's takeaway. If talk length or audience expertise is unclear and would change scope, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Save the deck as a PPTX file and list the file name in chat.

placeholders.label: Presentation context (e.g., conference talk, lab meeting, journal club)

name: Create a Conference Poster
description: Scientific poster layout from paper or results
prompt: Design a scientific conference poster from the uploaded paper or results. Include title and authors (with affiliations), introduction/background, methods, key results with figures, conclusions/takeaways, and references plus contact/acknowledgements. Organize content in a clear column-based grid with an obvious reading order, a prominent title band, and a one-line take-home message. Use large readable type (title ~72-100 pt, headings ~36-48 pt, body ~24-32 pt), a colorblind-safe palette, strong contrast, generous whitespace, and figures reused from the paper at high resolution with honest scales; never invent data. Default to A0 portrait (841 x 1189 mm) unless told otherwise. If poster size, orientation, or the single key message is unclear and would change the layout, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Save the poster as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable PPTX (or LaTeX source) and list the file names in chat.

name: Create an Infographic
description: Visual explainer for broader audiences
prompt: Create an infographic that explains the key findings from the uploaded paper or data for a broad, non-specialist audience, using only real figures and results from the source; never invent numbers. Tell one clear story with a strong title, a logical top-to-bottom reading flow, and 3-5 sections linked by visual cues. Use simple icons, large readable numbers, and minimal jargon (define unavoidable terms), with simple honest charts and no misleading scales. Apply a colorblind-safe palette, consistent iconography, generous whitespace, legible fonts, and good text/background contrast for accessibility. Default to a vertical portrait layout suitable for web and print unless told otherwise. If the audience or single key message is unclear and would change the design, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Save the infographic as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable SVG and list the file names in chat.

name: Create a Scientific Schematic
description: Mechanistic or conceptual diagram of a process
prompt: Create a scientific schematic diagram illustrating: {concept}. Clearly show the key components, their relationships, and the flow of the process using arrows and consistent visual encoding (shape/color = type of entity), following standard conventions for the relevant field. Establish a clear reading order, use clean lines, even spacing, and concise labels with units where applicable; never invent mechanisms or quantities not implied by the concept. Apply a colorblind-safe palette, legible fonts, high contrast, and minimal clutter so it remains readable when scaled to journal column width. Add a short caption stating what the schematic depicts. If important details (scale, level of mechanistic detail, target journal style) are ambiguous and would change the result, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Save the schematic as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable source (SVG or Mermaid file) and list the file names in chat.

name: Create a Flowchart
description: Experimental or analytical workflow diagram
prompt: Create a detailed flowchart for: {workflow}. Show all decision points, parallel processes, inputs/outputs, and terminal outcomes, with a clear top-to-bottom or left-to-right reading order and labeled connectors (e.g., yes/no on decision branches). Use standard symbols: rectangles for processes, diamonds for decisions, ovals for start/end, and parallelograms for inputs/outputs. Apply consistent color coding for different phases with a colorblind-safe palette and a small legend, plus legible fonts, even spacing, and minimal crossing lines. Keep labels concise and accurate to the described workflow; do not invent steps. If the workflow has ambiguous branching or scope that would change the diagram, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Save the flowchart as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable source (Mermaid file or SVG) and list the file names in chat.

name: Create a Research Timeline
description: Visual timeline of project milestones and deliverables
prompt: Create a visual research timeline for a project on: {project}, spanning {duration}. Lay out key milestones, deliverables, and work packages along a time axis in Gantt-chart style, showing durations, dependencies (linked bars or arrows), and go/no-go decision points as distinct markers. Use color-coded phases with a colorblind-safe palette and a clear legend, a labeled time axis with sensible tick intervals matching {duration}, and legible fonts with even row spacing. Keep the structure realistic and consistent; do not invent milestones beyond what the project implies. Ensure it reads clearly left-to-right and stays legible at report and slide sizes. If the milestone list or phase breakdown is unspecified and would change the result, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the timeline as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable source (SVG or the plotting script) and list the file names in chat.

name: Create a Heatmap
description: Heatmap visualization from matrix data
prompt: Create a publication-quality heatmap from the uploaded matrix data, using the real values only. (1) Apply appropriate clustering (hierarchical or k-means) to rows and columns if it aids interpretation, (2) choose a perceptually uniform colormap (sequential for one-directional data, diverging centered at a meaningful midpoint for signed data), (3) add dendrograms when clustered, (4) label rows, columns, and axes clearly with legible fonts, (5) include a color bar with units and an honest, non-misleading scale. Consider standardizing or log-transforming only when justified and state it. Use a colorblind-safe palette, annotate cells only if the matrix is small enough to stay readable, and keep it legible at journal column width with no chartjunk. If clustering choice or normalization is ambiguous and would change the result, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Save the heatmap as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable source (SVG or PDF) and list the file names in chat. {instructions}

name: Create a Network Diagram
description: Network or interaction graph visualization
prompt: Create a network visualization from the uploaded data using only the real nodes and edges present. (1) Parse nodes and edges (and edge weights/direction if available), (2) choose a layout that fits the structure (force-directed, circular, or hierarchical) and avoid excessive overlap, (3) size nodes by {metric}, (4) color-code by community or cluster using a colorblind-safe palette, (5) label key nodes legibly without cluttering, (6) include a clear legend explaining size and color encodings, plus edge weight if shown. Keep fonts legible, reduce hairball density (filter or bundle edges if needed), and avoid chartjunk so it stays readable at journal column width. If node count is large or the sizing/coloring choice is ambiguous and would change the result, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Make it publication-ready and save it as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable source (SVG or the plotting script), then list the file names in chat.

name: Create a Geographic Map
description: Geographic or spatial data visualization
prompt: Create a geographic visualization from the uploaded data using only its real coordinates and values. (1) Plot points or regions on a map projection appropriate to the extent and purpose (e.g., equal-area for area comparisons), (2) encode quantitative values with a perceptually uniform color gradient or proportionally sized markers, classifying values honestly (state the binning method), (3) add a legend, scale bar, and north arrow where relevant, (4) include supporting geographic features (borders, coastlines, rivers) for context without clutter, (5) use a colorblind-safe palette, legible labels, and good land/water contrast. Avoid misleading area or color scaling and keep it readable at journal column width. If the projection, value field, or extent is ambiguous and would change the result, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Make it publication-ready and save it as a high-resolution PNG plus an editable source (SVG or the plotting script), then list the file names in chat.

name: Create an Interactive Dashboard
description: Interactive multi-panel data dashboard
prompt: Create an interactive data dashboard from the uploaded dataset using Plotly, working only from the real data. Include: (1) a summary statistics panel, (2) key distributions as interactive histograms, (3) correlation scatter plots with hover details, (4) a filterable data table, and (5) a time series panel if temporal data exists. Lay out panels in a clear grid with a logical reading order, descriptive titles, axis labels with units, and linked or consistent filters. Use a colorblind-safe palette, legible fonts, honest non-truncated scales, and informative hover tooltips; keep it responsive and uncluttered. Handle missing values and large row counts gracefully (sampling or aggregation) and note any such handling. If essential choices (key variables, grouping, or audience) are ambiguous and would change the result, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. Save the dashboard as a self-contained HTML file and list the file name in chat.

name: Explore My Data
description: EDA: distributions, correlations, missing values, and visualizations
prompt: Perform a rigorous exploratory data analysis on the uploaded dataset. (1) Inspect structure: rows, columns, dtypes, and memory; preview head/tail. (2) Quantify missingness per column and visualize its pattern; note likely mechanisms. (3) Compute descriptive statistics for numeric and categorical features, including cardinality and class balance. (4) Visualize distributions (histograms, boxplots) and pairwise relationships; compute a correlation matrix (Pearson and Spearman). (5) Flag anomalies, outliers, duplicates, constant columns, skew, and potential leakage or data-quality issues. State assumptions if context is missing. Set and report a random seed. Deliverables: save figures as PNG, summary tables as CSV, and a concise findings report as Markdown to the sandbox; then summarize key patterns in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Statistical Analysis
description: Appropriate tests, p-values, effect sizes, and assumption checks
prompt: Run appropriate statistical analyses on the uploaded data. First inspect schema, missingness, distributions, and group sizes. If the comparison, grouping, or outcome is ambiguous and would materially change conclusions, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. For each analysis: (1) state the null and alternative hypotheses, (2) check test assumptions (normality, variance homogeneity, independence) and pick a suitable parametric or non-parametric test, (3) run the test, (4) report test statistics, exact p-values, confidence intervals, and standardized effect sizes, (5) correct for multiple comparisons where applicable, (6) interpret results honestly with caveats. Set and report a random seed. Deliverables: save figures as PNG, a results table as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations. {instructions}

placeholders.label: Specific analyses or comparisons to run (optional)

name: Reproduce an Analysis
description: Re-run a paper's methods on provided data and verify results
prompt: Reproduce the analysis described in the uploaded paper using the provided data. (1) Extract the methods: outcomes, predictors, inclusion/exclusion criteria, transformations, and exact statistical procedures. (2) Verify the data matches the paper's described sample (n, variables, units); document discrepancies in cohort or preprocessing. (3) Implement the same tests and models step by step, checking their assumptions. (4) Report test statistics, p-values, confidence intervals, and effect sizes, then compare side by side with the reported figures and tables. (5) Quantify and explain any discrepancies (data version, missing detail, software differences); state assumptions where the methods are underspecified. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save comparison tables as CSV, figures as PNG, and a Markdown reproducibility report to the sandbox; summarize agreement in chat, name files, and note limitations.

name: Clean & Transform Data
description: Wrangle messy datasets into analysis-ready format
prompt: Clean and transform the uploaded dataset into an analysis-ready format. (1) Profile the raw data: schema, dtypes, missingness, duplicates, cardinality, and distributions. (2) Standardize column names and fix data types (dates, numerics, categoricals). (3) Handle missing values with a justified strategy (drop, impute, or flag) and document the rationale. (4) Remove or reconcile duplicates and inconsistent categories. (5) Investigate outliers and decide to keep, cap, or transform them with justification, avoiding silent data loss. (6) Apply needed transformations (scaling, encoding) only where appropriate, avoiding leakage. Set and report a random seed. Deliverables: save the cleaned dataset as CSV plus a before/after data-quality summary as CSV and a Markdown changelog documenting every step to the sandbox; summarize key changes in chat, name files, and note limitations.

name: Build a Predictive Model
description: Train and evaluate ML models on uploaded data
prompt: Build a predictive model on the uploaded data. Target variable: {target}. If the task type (regression vs classification) or evaluation metric is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Explore data, check missingness, class balance, and screen for leakage. (2) Make a train/validation/test split (or nested CV) and fit preprocessing inside the pipeline to prevent leakage. (3) Establish a simple baseline, then train appropriate models, tuning only on validation/CV. (4) Evaluate on the held-out test set with task-appropriate metrics (accuracy, AUC, F1, RMSE, MAE) plus uncertainty and calibration where relevant. (5) Interpret with SHAP/feature importance. (6) Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save the trained model, predictions CSV, figures PNG, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Time Series Analysis
description: Trend decomposition, forecasting, and anomaly detection
prompt: Analyze the uploaded time series data. (1) Parse and sort timestamps, check sampling frequency, gaps, and duplicates; visualize the series. (2) Test for trend, seasonality, and stationarity (ADF/KPSS), differencing as needed. (3) Decompose into trend/seasonal/residual components. (4) Hold out the most recent window for validation; establish a naive/seasonal-naive baseline, then fit appropriate models (ARIMA/SARIMA, Prophet, or ML-based), tuning on validation only to avoid leakage. (5) Generate forecasts for {horizon} with prediction intervals and backtested error metrics (MAE, RMSE, MAPE). (6) Detect anomalies and report residual diagnostics. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save forecast PNGs, a forecast/anomaly CSV, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize accuracy in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Survival Analysis
description: Kaplan-Meier curves, Cox regression, and hazard modeling
prompt: Perform survival analysis on the uploaded data. First verify the time and event (censoring) columns and check follow-up and censoring patterns. (1) Generate Kaplan-Meier curves with confidence bands for key groups and report median survival. (2) Run log-rank tests for group comparisons. (3) Fit a Cox proportional hazards model with covariates: {covariates}, checking multicollinearity and screening for leakage. (4) Test the proportional hazards assumption (Schoenfeld residuals) and address violations if found. (5) Report hazard ratios with 95% CIs and p-values, plus model fit (concordance). (6) Create forest plots. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save figures as PNG, a results table as CSV, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Meta-Analysis
description: Pool effect sizes across studies with heterogeneity assessment
prompt: Conduct a meta-analysis using the uploaded study data. (1) Extract or verify per-study effect sizes and standard errors/variances; confirm the effect metric and direction are consistent. (2) Fit both fixed-effects and random-effects (e.g., DerSimonian-Laird or REML) models and report the pooled estimate with 95% CI and a prediction interval. (3) Assess heterogeneity (I-squared, Cochran's Q with p-value, tau-squared) and interpret it. (4) Generate a forest plot with study weights. (5) Create a funnel plot and run Egger's test to assess publication bias. (6) Run sensitivity (leave-one-out) and pre-specified subgroup/meta-regression analyses. State assumptions where study detail is missing. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save plots as PNG, a pooled-results CSV, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Power Analysis
description: Sample size calculation and power curves
prompt: Perform a power analysis for a {study_design}. Parameters: expected effect size = {effect_size}, significance level = {alpha} (default 0.05 if unspecified). (1) State the test, hypotheses, and the effect-size metric, justifying the assumed value. (2) Calculate the required sample size for 80% and 90% power, noting any allocation ratio or number of groups. (3) Generate power curves across a range of plausible effect sizes and sample sizes. (4) Adjust the significance threshold for multiple comparisons if applicable and recompute. (5) Run a sensitivity analysis showing how required n shifts with effect-size and dropout assumptions, and recommend a target sample with attrition buffer. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save power-curve PNGs, a sample-size table as CSV, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize recommendations in chat, name files, and state limitations.

placeholders.label: Study design (e.g., two-sample t-test, ANOVA, logistic regression)

name: Bayesian Analysis
description: Bayesian inference with posterior distributions and credible intervals
prompt: Perform Bayesian analysis on the uploaded data. First inspect schema, missingness, and distributions. (1) Define the generative model and justified priors for {parameters}; state the likelihood and any reparameterization, and run prior predictive checks. (2) Run MCMC sampling (NUTS/HMC) with multiple chains. (3) Check convergence: R-hat near 1, sufficient ESS, trace plots, and divergences. (4) Report posterior means/medians with 95% credible intervals and visualize posteriors. (5) Compute Bayes factors or information criteria (WAIC/LOO) if comparing models. (6) Run posterior predictive checks against observed data. State assumptions where priors are uncertain and report seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save diagnostic and posterior PNGs, a posterior-summary CSV, the trace/inference artifact, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Dimensionality Reduction
description: PCA, t-SNE, UMAP for high-dimensional data visualization
prompt: Apply dimensionality reduction to the uploaded high-dimensional dataset. (1) Inspect schema, missingness, and feature scales; impute and standardize features as needed. (2) Run PCA and report variance explained per component with a scree plot. (3) Apply t-SNE and UMAP with documented, tuned parameters (perplexity, n_neighbors, min_dist), noting sensitivity. (4) Color points by {label} if available. (5) Compare methods side by side and assess which preserves structure, identifying clusters or outlier groups. (6) Quantify embedding quality (e.g., trustworthiness or silhouette on clusters). Set and report a random seed and key package versions for reproducibility. Deliverables: save embedding PNGs, reduced coordinates as CSV, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize findings in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Clustering Analysis
description: Unsupervised clustering with multiple algorithms
prompt: Perform clustering analysis on the uploaded dataset. (1) Inspect schema and missingness, then preprocess and scale features appropriately, avoiding leakage. (2) Determine the optimal cluster count using multiple criteria (elbow, silhouette, gap statistic). (3) Apply k-means, hierarchical, and DBSCAN, reporting parameters. (4) Compare results across methods using internal validation metrics (silhouette, Davies-Bouldin) and stability checks, then select the best. (5) Characterize each cluster by distinguishing features with summary statistics and significance where useful. (6) Visualize clusters in UMAP/t-SNE space. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save cluster-assignment CSV, figures as PNG, and a Markdown report describing each cluster to the sandbox; summarize key segments in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Regression Analysis
description: Linear, logistic, or mixed-effects regression modeling
prompt: Fit a {regression_type} regression model on the uploaded data. Outcome: {outcome}, predictors: {predictors}. (1) Inspect missingness and distributions, then check assumptions appropriate to the model (linearity, residual normality, homoscedasticity, independence, multicollinearity via VIF; for logistic/Poisson check separation, dispersion, and link). (2) Fit the model, using robust or clustered standard errors if assumptions are violated. (3) Report coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values, plus interpretable effect sizes (e.g., odds/rate ratios). (4) Assess fit (R-squared/pseudo-R-squared, AIC, BIC) and validate on held-out data if predictive. (5) Generate diagnostic plots (residuals, QQ, influence). (6) Interpret key findings and caveats. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save diagnostic PNGs, a coefficient table as CSV, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: ANOVA / Group Comparisons
description: One-way, two-way, or repeated-measures ANOVA
prompt: Perform ANOVA on the uploaded data. Factor(s): {factors}, outcome: {outcome}. (1) Inspect group sizes, balance, and missingness, then check assumptions (residual normality via Shapiro/QQ, homogeneity of variance via Levene's, independence). (2) Run the appropriate ANOVA (one-way, two-way with interaction, or repeated measures), using a robust alternative (Welch/Kruskal-Wallis) if assumptions fail. (3) Report F-statistics, degrees of freedom, and exact p-values. (4) Run post-hoc pairwise comparisons (e.g., Tukey HSD) with multiple-comparison correction. (5) Calculate effect sizes (eta-squared or partial eta-squared) with interpretation. (6) Create bar/box plots with significance markers and CIs. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save plots as PNG, an ANOVA/post-hoc table as CSV, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Correlation Network
description: Build and visualize correlation-based networks
prompt: Build a correlation network from the uploaded dataset. (1) Inspect schema, missingness, and feature scales; choose Pearson, Spearman, or partial correlations with justification. (2) Compute pairwise correlations with p-values, correct for multiple comparisons (e.g., FDR), and retain edges passing p < {threshold} (default 0.05). (3) Construct the network graph, optionally weighting edges by correlation strength. (4) Identify hubs and detect communities (e.g., modularity-based). (5) Visualize with node sizes proportional to connectivity and edges colored by sign. (6) Report network statistics (density, modularity, average clustering coefficient, degree distribution). Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save the network PNG, an edge/node table as CSV, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize key hubs and communities in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Literature Search
description: Find and summarize relevant papers on a topic
prompt: Search the literature on the topic: {topic}. Query multiple real databases (e.g., PubMed, Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, Web of Science, arXiv) via web search. (1) Find the most relevant and highly cited papers, prioritizing recent work while including landmark studies. (2) Summarize the key findings of each, distinguishing primary research from reviews and other secondary sources. (3) Identify major themes and points of consensus, (4) note controversies and conflicting evidence, and (5) highlight gaps in current knowledge. Cite every claim with a real, verifiable reference (title, authors, year, venue, and DOI or URL); never fabricate citations or DOIs, and flag anything you could not verify. Report your search scope, databases, and date. If the topic is too broad or ambiguous to scope, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save a structured bibliography as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the findings briefly in chat.

name: Summarize a Paper
description: Structured summary: objective, methods, findings, and significance
prompt: Provide a structured, faithful summary of the uploaded paper: (1) Research objective and hypothesis, (2) Study design and methods, (3) Key findings and results (with the main quantitative outcomes), (4) Limitations acknowledged by the authors plus any you notice, (5) Conclusions and significance, (6) How this fits into the broader field. Represent the authors' claims accurately and distinguish their evidence-backed findings from their speculation; do not add facts that are not in the paper, and note any claims that seem under-supported. Keep it concise but thorough. If the paper is unreadable or appears truncated, say so rather than guessing. Save the summary as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and give a short recap in chat.

name: Compare Papers
description: Side-by-side analysis of multiple papers
prompt: Compare and contrast the uploaded papers with a structured analysis covering: (1) Research questions and objectives, (2) Methodological approaches, (3) Key findings, noting agreements and direct contradictions, (4) Strengths and weaknesses of each, (5) Which provides stronger evidence and why, judged on study design, sample size, rigor, and reproducibility. Present a comparative table where it aids clarity. Represent each paper accurately, cite specific sections or figures when stating a claim, and do not invent results or papers that were not provided. Flag where the papers are not directly comparable (different populations, endpoints, or methods). If it is unclear which papers or what comparison axis matter most, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the comparison as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the key differences briefly in chat.

name: Research Landscape Map
description: Visual map of a field's key themes, groups, and trends
prompt: Create a research landscape map for the field of: {topic}. Using web search across multiple databases, identify (1) the major research themes and sub-areas, (2) key research groups and labs, (3) landmark papers, (4) current trends, (5) emerging directions, and (6) funding patterns. Prioritize recent and highly cited work, distinguish established findings from speculative directions, and cite every group, paper, or claim with a real, verifiable reference; never fabricate citations or affiliations, and flag anything unverified. Report your search scope and date, and note gaps and open debates. Visualize the landscape as a structured diagram or concept map (e.g., generated with code) and save the image to the sandbox. If the field is too broad to scope, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the map and a referenced writeup as well-named files in the sandbox for preview and download, and recap it briefly in chat.

name: Systematic Review Protocol
description: Design a systematic review with PRISMA methodology
prompt: Design a rigorous systematic review protocol for: {question}, following PRISMA guidelines. (1) Formulate the PICO question, (2) Define explicit inclusion and exclusion criteria, (3) Create reproducible search strategies with full Boolean queries for PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus, (4) Describe screening (title/abstract then full text, with dual reviewers) and data extraction procedures, (5) Plan risk-of-bias and quality assessment using appropriate validated tools (e.g., Cochrane RoB 2, Newcastle-Ottawa), (6) Outline the analysis plan, including meta-analysis and heterogeneity assessment if applicable. Reference real methodological guidance and reporting standards; never fabricate citations, and note where pilot searches are needed to refine terms. If the population, intervention, or outcomes are ambiguous and would change the design, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the protocol as a well-named Markdown file with a references list in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Citation Analysis
description: Analyze citation patterns and impact of a body of work
prompt: Perform a citation analysis on the topic: {topic}, using real bibliometric sources (e.g., Semantic Scholar, OpenAlex, Google Scholar) via web search. (1) Identify the most cited papers and their citation trajectories over time, (2) map citation networks between key papers, (3) identify citation bursts signaling rapidly emerging subtopics, (4) report h-index and other metrics for key authors, clearly stating the data source and date, (5) visualize the citation network with code and save the figure to the sandbox. Cite every paper, author, and metric with a real, verifiable reference; never fabricate citations, DOIs, or counts, and flag any figures you could not verify (note that citation counts vary by database). If the topic or paper set is ambiguous, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the analysis and visualization as well-named files in the sandbox for preview and download, and recap it briefly in chat.

name: Find Public Datasets
description: Locate relevant public datasets for a research question
prompt: Find publicly available datasets relevant to: {topic} by searching across major repositories (e.g., GEO, ArrayExpress, TCGA, UK Biobank, Data Commons, Zenodo, Figshare, Kaggle, and domain-specific archives) via web search. For each dataset: (1) describe its contents and what it measures, (2) report sample size and key variables, (3) note the data format and approximate size, (4) provide the verified access URL, (5) list license and usage restrictions plus any access or ethics approvals required. Confirm each link resolves and never invent dataset names, sizes, or URLs; flag anything you could not verify. Prioritize well-documented, actively maintained, and recent datasets, and note quality or coverage caveats. If the data type or scope is ambiguous, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save a structured dataset catalog as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the top options briefly in chat.

name: Track Research Trends
description: Analyze publication trends and emerging topics in a field
prompt: Analyze research trends in the field of: {field} over the past {years} years using real bibliometric data (e.g., OpenAlex, Semantic Scholar, PubMed) via web search. (1) Plot publication volume over time, (2) identify the fastest-growing subtopics, (3) map the geographic distribution of research output, (4) identify top-publishing institutions and authors, (5) highlight emerging methodologies, (6) project near-future trends, clearly labeling these as extrapolations rather than facts. Generate the plots with code (e.g., matplotlib) and save the figures to the sandbox. State your data source, query, and extraction date, cite key papers with verifiable references, and never fabricate counts or citations; flag gaps where coverage is incomplete. If the field is too broad to scope, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the analysis and figures as well-named files in the sandbox for preview and download, and recap the key trends briefly in chat.

name: Find Recent Preprints
description: Search bioRxiv, arXiv, and other preprint servers
prompt: Search for recent preprints on: {topic}, checking bioRxiv, medRxiv, arXiv, and other relevant preprint servers via web search. (1) List the most relevant preprints from the past {months} months, prioritizing recency and relevance, (2) summarize each paper's main finding in 2-3 sentences, (3) note which have since been peer-reviewed or formally published (with the journal and DOI where available), (4) highlight any contradictory findings or methodological concerns. Stress that preprints are not peer-reviewed and should be interpreted with caution. Cite every preprint with a real, verifiable link or DOI; never fabricate titles, authors, or DOIs, and flag anything you could not confirm. Report your search scope and date. If the topic is ambiguous, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save a structured list as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the highlights briefly in chat.

name: Patent Landscape
description: Analyze patent filings and IP landscape for a technology
prompt: Analyze the patent landscape for: {technology} using real sources (e.g., USPTO, Google Patents, Espacenet, WIPO PATENTSCOPE) via web search. (1) Search the major patent databases, (2) identify key patent holders and filing trends over time, (3) map patent families and citations, (4) identify potential freedom-to-operate concerns, (5) highlight recent filings and emerging IP areas, (6) summarize the competitive IP landscape. Cite each patent with its number, assignee, and filing/grant date, and never fabricate patent numbers or claims; flag anything unverified. Report your search scope and date, and add a clear caveat that this is informational analysis, not legal advice, and a professional patent search and counsel are needed for any FTO or filing decision. If the technology scope is ambiguous, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the analysis as a well-named Markdown file with a patent reference list in the sandbox for preview and download, and recap it briefly in chat.

name: Compare Methods
description: Systematic comparison of alternative methods for a task
prompt: Systematically compare the methods used for: {task}, grounded in the literature via web search across multiple sources. (1) Identify the main methods used for this task, prioritizing established and recent approaches, (2) compare them on clear criteria: accuracy/performance, computational cost, ease of use, key assumptions, data and sample-size requirements, and interpretability, (3) summarize which method is best under which conditions, (4) present a comparison table, (5) provide concrete recommendations with their trade-offs. Cite every method and performance claim with a real, verifiable reference, distinguishing benchmarked results from author claims; never fabricate citations or numbers, and note where evidence is thin or context-dependent. Report your search scope and date. If the task constraints (data type, scale, goals) are unclear and would change the recommendation, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the comparison as a well-named Markdown file with a references list in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the recommendation briefly in chat.

name: Write a Specific Aim
description: Draft a grant Specific Aim with significance and approach
prompt: Draft a one-page Specific Aims page for a grant proposal on: {topic}, targeting {agency} and following that mechanism's conventions and length. Structure it as: (1) an opening paragraph establishing significance and the critical knowledge gap; (2) a statement of the long-term goal, the overall objective of this proposal, and the central hypothesis with its basis; (3) 2-3 specific aims, each with a brief rationale, the approach, and the expected outcome, written as testable objectives that are related but not mutually dependent; (4) a closing paragraph on expected impact and how it advances the field. Search the literature to ground motivating claims in real, verifiable sources; never fabricate citations, statistics, or preliminary data, and flag with [VERIFY] anything unconfirmed. If the hypothesis or scope is ambiguous in a way that changes the aims, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the Specific Aims page (with references) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

placeholders.label: Funding agency (e.g., NIH R01, NSF CAREER)

name: Write a Research Plan
description: Hypotheses, methods, timeline, and expected outcomes
prompt: Create a detailed research plan for investigating: {topic}. Include: (1) Background and rationale grounded in prior work; (2) clear, testable hypotheses; (3) experimental design and methods sufficient to judge feasibility and rigor, including key variables, controls, sample-size/power considerations, and analysis approach; (4) expected outcomes plus interpretation and concrete alternative strategies if results differ; (5) a timeline with milestones and go/no-go decision points; (6) required resources and personnel. Where you cite evidence, use real, verifiable sources and never fabricate citations, preliminary data, or statistics; flag anything needing a real reference with [VERIFY]. Note the main scientific and feasibility risks and caveats. If the research question or scope is ambiguous in a way that materially changes the plan, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions explicitly. Save the research plan as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and give a brief summary in chat.

name: Draft a Budget Justification
description: Budget narrative for a grant proposal
prompt: Draft a budget justification narrative for a grant proposal on: {topic}, with a total budget target of {budget}. Organize by standard categories: personnel (PI, co-investigators, postdocs, students, staff, with roles, percent effort, and contribution to the work), equipment, supplies/consumables, travel, publication/other direct costs, and a note on indirect costs if relevant. Justify each line item specifically in terms of the proposed research rather than with generic language, and tie effort and purchases to particular aims or tasks. Use realistic, clearly-labeled estimates; never present invented figures as institution-verified, and flag rates, salaries, or quotes that the applicant must confirm with [VERIFY]. Keep totals internally consistent with the target. If essential details (duration, key personnel, or major equipment) are missing or ambiguous, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the budget justification as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write a Biosketch
description: NIH or NSF-style biosketch for a PI
prompt: Draft a biosketch for {name} for a {agency} proposal on {topic}, following the current {agency} biosketch format, structure, and length limits. Include: (1) a Personal Statement connecting the candidate's expertise and prior work directly to this project; (2) Positions, Scientific Appointments, and Honors in reverse-chronological order; (3) Contributions to Science (up to 5, each a short narrative with up to 4 representative publications); (4) Research Support, current and completed, if applicable. Use only information the user provides or that is publicly verifiable; never fabricate degrees, positions, honors, publications, or grant numbers, and clearly mark with [PROVIDE] or [VERIFY] any field you cannot fill from given material. Write in a confident, specific tone that frames the candidate as uniquely suited to the work. If essential biographical details are missing, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise leave clearly-labeled placeholders. Save the biosketch as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write Significance Section
description: Establish the importance and impact of proposed research
prompt: Write the Significance section for a grant proposal on: {topic}. Build a persuasive, evidence-based case that: (1) establishes the scope, burden, or importance of the problem; (2) reviews what is currently known and pinpoints the critical gap or barrier the field faces; (3) explains how the proposed research will fill that gap and why it is timely; (4) describes the expected impact on scientific knowledge, methods, or practice if the work succeeds. Search the literature and cite real, verifiable statistics and prior findings to motivate the work; never fabricate citations or numbers, and flag with [VERIFY] anything you cannot confirm. Write persuasively without overstating certainty, and keep the focus on the gap and payoff. If the scope or intended impact is ambiguous in a way that changes the framing, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the Significance section (with references) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write Innovation Section
description: Articulate what is novel about the proposed approach
prompt: Write the Innovation section for a grant proposal on: {topic}. Make a specific, defensible case for novelty by describing: (1) how the proposed approach departs from and improves on current practice or the status quo; (2) the new technologies, methods, models, or reagents being developed or applied; (3) the novel conceptual framework, hypothesis, or perspective introduced; (4) why this innovation matters for advancing the field and shifting current paradigms. Be concrete and contrast with existing methods rather than making generic claims like 'first-ever'; where you assert that something is new or better, ground it in real, verifiable literature and flag with [VERIFY] anything unconfirmed. Never fabricate citations or prior-art comparisons. If the distinctive approach is ambiguous in a way that changes the argument, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the Innovation section (with references) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write Research Strategy / Approach
description: Detailed experimental plan for a grant proposal
prompt: Write the Approach/Research Strategy section for a grant on: {topic}, formatted for {agency} conventions and length. For each specific aim, cover: (1) rationale and the hypothesis being tested; (2) preliminary data supporting feasibility (clearly labeled, using only data the user provides; never fabricate results or figures); (3) detailed experimental design including key variables, controls, sample size/power, and rigor and reproducibility measures; (4) expected results and how they will be interpreted; (5) potential pitfalls with concrete alternative approaches; (6) a timeline with milestones. Ensure aims are logically connected but not fatally dependent on one another. Cite real, verifiable sources for any external claim and flag unconfirmed items with [VERIFY]. If aims, design, or available preliminary data are ambiguous in a way that materially changes the strategy, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the section (with references) as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Create a Project Timeline
description: Gantt chart and milestone plan for a grant
prompt: Create a detailed project timeline for a {duration} project on: {topic}. Include: (1) a Gantt chart laying out all tasks grouped by aim across the full duration; (2) milestones and deliverables with target dates; (3) go/no-go decision points; (4) personnel assignments per task; (5) dependencies and critical-path relationships between tasks. Render the Gantt chart as an actual figure (e.g., with matplotlib), use realistic, clearly-stated task durations, and note key assumptions and scheduling risks. Base tasks on the proposed work rather than inventing unrelated activities, and flag anything that needs the user's input with [CONFIRM]. If the aims or scope are too vague to schedule meaningfully, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the Gantt chart image plus a written timeline narrative (milestones, decision points, dependencies) together as well-named files in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the plan briefly in chat.

name: Write a Data Management Plan
description: DMP for grant compliance (NIH, NSF, etc.)
prompt: Write a Data Management Plan (or Data Management and Sharing Plan) for a {agency} grant on: {topic}, following the current {agency} policy and required format and length. Cover: (1) the types, formats, and estimated volume of data generated; (2) data and metadata standards; (3) access, sharing, and reuse policies, including the repository and timeline for sharing; (4) archiving and long-term preservation; (5) privacy, security, consent, and any human-subjects or proprietary considerations; (6) roles, responsibilities, and oversight. Look up the relevant policy and state the version you relied on; never invent repository names, identifiers, or policy clauses, and flag with [VERIFY] anything you cannot confirm. Note any compliance gaps or caveats. If essential details (data types or sharing constraints) are missing or ambiguous, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the plan as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write Broader Impacts
description: NSF broader impacts or NIH dissemination plan
prompt: Write a Broader Impacts section (NSF) or Dissemination Plan for a proposal on: {topic}, following the relevant funder's review criteria. Include concrete, well-justified activities for: (1) education and training (students, mentees, curriculum); (2) broadening participation of groups underrepresented in STEM, with specific recruitment and support mechanisms; (3) public engagement and outreach; (4) potential societal, economic, or health benefits tied to the science; (5) dissemination through open-access publication, data and code sharing, and community resources. Propose realistic, measurable activities with assessment metrics rather than vague aspirations, and tie them to the project's resources and setting. Do not fabricate partnerships, programs, or statistics; flag anything the applicant must confirm with [VERIFY]. If key context (institution programs or target audiences) is missing in a way that changes the plan, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the section as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Risk Mitigation Plan
description: Identify risks and alternative strategies for a project
prompt: Create a risk mitigation plan for a research project on: {topic}. For each aim: (1) identify the key scientific, technical, and operational risks; (2) rate each by likelihood and impact (e.g., low/medium/high) with a brief rationale; (3) describe specific mitigation strategies and credible alternative approaches that keep the project on track; (4) define early warning indicators and the decision/trigger point for switching to a fallback; (5) summarize everything in a risk matrix visualization (likelihood vs. impact) rendered as an actual figure. Base risks on the real demands of the proposed work rather than generic boilerplate, and avoid overstating or understating severity. Where a risk rests on an external assumption, state it and flag unverified claims with [VERIFY]. If the aims or scope are too vague to assess risk meaningfully, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the risk matrix image and a written risk register together as well-named files in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize the top risks in chat.

name: Facilities & Equipment
description: Describe available resources and infrastructure
prompt: Write a Facilities, Equipment, and Other Resources section for a grant on: {topic} at {institution}, following the conventional funder format (descriptive narrative, no direct cost figures). Describe, with subheadings: (1) laboratory space and major equipment relevant to the aims; (2) computational resources (clusters, storage, software); (3) core facilities and shared instrumentation; (4) animal, clinical, or human-subjects facilities if applicable; (5) institutional support, collaborations, and intellectual environment; (6) office and meeting space. Frame each resource around how it enables the proposed research. Use only resources the user provides or that are publicly verifiable for that institution; never invent instruments, core facilities, or capabilities, and clearly mark anything to be confirmed with [VERIFY] or [PROVIDE]. If key facility details are missing in a way that materially affects the section, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise leave labeled placeholders. Save the section as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Plain Language Summary
description: Rewrite for non-specialist audiences or press
prompt: Rewrite the uploaded paper or abstract in plain language for a curious non-specialist reader (roughly an 8th-grade reading level), suitable for a press release or public-facing summary. (1) Read the source carefully and identify the key findings and their real-world significance. (2) Convey them accurately in 2-3 paragraphs without distorting or overclaiming. (3) Replace or briefly define any jargon, and use a concrete everyday analogy where it genuinely aids understanding. (4) Frame uncertainty and limitations honestly rather than hyping results. If the intended audience or venue is unclear and would materially change the tone, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumption. Save the finished summary as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox so the user can preview and download it, and give a short recap in chat.

name: Write a Blog Post
description: Engaging science blog from paper or results
prompt: Write an engaging scientific blog post (800-1200 words) based on the uploaded paper or results, for an interested general audience. (1) Open with a compelling hook tied to a real problem or surprising fact. (2) Explain the science clearly using examples and concrete analogies, defining any necessary jargon. (3) Stay faithful to the source: do not overstate findings, and note limitations and open questions honestly. (4) Close with why this matters and what comes next. If you add outside context, verify it via web search and cite real, verifiable sources; never fabricate references. If the desired tone, publication, or audience is unclear and would change the piece, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the post as a well-named Markdown file (with any references listed) in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Draft a Protocol

Base any reagent or method specifics on established, verifiable references and cite them; never invent catalog numbers or citations, and flag any step where values must be validated by the user. If the scale, organism/system, or instrument is unclear and would change the steps, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the protocol as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and recap it briefly in chat.

Represent the science faithfully, avoid overclaiming, and state limitations honestly; do not invent affiliations, funding, or quotes. Verify any added external facts via web search and cite real sources. If the institution or spokesperson is unclear and would change the framing, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the release as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

Keep each post under 280 characters for Twitter/X. Stay faithful to the source, avoid hype or overclaiming, define or skip jargon, and note key caveats honestly rather than implying certainty. Do not fabricate stats or quotes; verify any added facts. Save the full thread as a well-named Markdown file in the sandbox for preview and download, and recap it briefly in chat.

Structure: (1) An engaging introduction with a hook, (2) Background context, (3) Main discussion points grounded in recent findings, (4) Interview-style questions and talking points, (5) A conclusion with key takeaways. Target roughly {duration} minutes of speaking. Search the web for current, credible sources, prioritize recent and well-cited work, define jargon on first use, and frame uncertainty honestly without overclaiming. Cite real sources in script notes and never fabricate references, quotes, or guest statements. If the show's audience or format is unclear and would change the script, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the script as a well-named Markdown file (with a sources list) in the sandbox for preview and download, and summarize it briefly in chat.

name: Write a Policy Brief

Include: (1) Executive summary, (2) Problem statement with data, (3) Key research findings, (4) Policy options with pros and cons, (5) Recommended actions, (6) Implementation considerations. Search the literature and authoritative sources across multiple databases, prioritize recent and highly cited work, distinguish primary studies from secondary summaries, and cite every claim with a real, verifiable reference; never fabricate citations or statistics. Report your search scope and date, and surface consensus, controversy, and evidence gaps honestly. Keep to 2-4 pages, in clear non-technical language for decision makers. If the jurisdiction or scope is unclear and would change the recommendations, ask one quick clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state your assumptions. Save the brief as a well-named Markdown file with a references list in the sandbox for preview and download, and recap it briefly in chat.
