name: Diagnostic Test Accuracy
description: Evaluate sensitivity, specificity, ROC for a diagnostic test
prompt: Evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of {test} for {condition} using the uploaded data. Pre-specify the reference standard and the positivity threshold; if these or the intended-use population are ambiguous, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first, otherwise state assumptions. (1) Calculate sensitivity and specificity with 95% confidence intervals. (2) Compute PPV and NPV at prevalence = {prevalence}, noting how predictive values shift with prevalence. (3) Generate an ROC curve with AUC and its 95% CI. (4) Determine an optimal cutoff (Youden's index) while reporting performance across clinically relevant thresholds. (5) Calculate positive and negative likelihood ratios with CIs. (6) Report following STARD, including a 2x2 table and handling of indeterminate results. Set a random seed and record package versions. Research and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save the ROC curve as PNG, result tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Treatment Comparison
description: Compare treatment outcomes between groups
prompt: Compare treatment outcomes between {treatments} using the uploaded clinical data. This is observational unless stated, so address confounding explicitly; if the primary outcome, follow-up window, or comparison is ambiguous, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first, otherwise state assumptions. (1) Check baseline balance between groups (standardized mean differences). (2) Analyze the primary outcome with appropriate tests, reporting absolute and relative effect sizes with 95% CIs. (3) Adjust for confounders using {method}, checking covariate overlap/positivity. (4) Calculate number needed to treat with a CI. (5) Analyze pre-specified secondary outcomes, correcting for multiple comparisons. (6) Run sensitivity analyses (e.g. alternative adjustment, E-value for unmeasured confounding). Set a random seed and record package versions. Research and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save forest/figures as PNG, tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report following STROBE; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Clinical Risk Prediction Model
description: Build a validated risk prediction model for clinical outcomes
prompt: Build a clinical risk prediction model for: {outcome}. If the prediction horizon, eligible population, or outcome definition is ambiguous in a way that changes the model, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Select candidate predictors from the uploaded data based on clinical rationale, avoiding leakage and respecting events-per-variable. (2) Handle missing data with multiple imputation. (3) Fit logistic regression and ML models. (4) Perform internal validation with bootstrapping (optimism-corrected estimates). (5) Assess calibration (calibration plot, slope/intercept) and discrimination (C-statistic/AUC) with 95% CIs, plus a decision-curve analysis. (6) Create a nomogram or risk calculator. Follow TRIPOD; set a random seed and record package versions. This is research and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save the calibration plot, ROC, and nomogram as PNG, performance tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Adverse Event Analysis
description: Analyze safety data from clinical studies
prompt: Analyze adverse events from the uploaded clinical safety data. If the safety population, exposure denominator, or treatment-emergent definition is ambiguous, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Tabulate AE frequencies by system organ class and preferred term (counts and patient incidence). (2) Compare AE rates between {groups} with risk differences and risk ratios, each with 95% confidence intervals; treat these as descriptive/exploratory and avoid over-interpreting many comparisons. (3) Identify dose-dependent AEs. (4) Analyze time-to-onset distributions. (5) Flag serious adverse events (SAEs) and discontinuations. (6) Generate standardized safety tables and forest plots following ICH E3. Set a random seed and record package versions. Research and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save forest plots and figures as PNG, safety tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis
description: Health economics evaluation of interventions
prompt: Perform a cost-effectiveness analysis comparing {interventions} for {condition}. If the perspective, time horizon, or effectiveness source is ambiguous in a way that changes conclusions, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions and cite sources. (1) Define the model structure (decision tree or Markov) with a stated cycle length and time horizon. (2) Populate with clinical effectiveness data, applying discounting to costs and outcomes. (3) Assign costs from the {perspective} perspective. (4) Calculate incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (ICERs) versus a defined comparator. (5) Run deterministic one-way and probabilistic sensitivity analysis (1000+ iterations), setting a random seed and recording package versions. (6) Generate a cost-effectiveness plane and acceptability curves, and report against willingness-to-pay thresholds. This is a research model and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save figures as PNG, model inputs/results as CSV, and a concise Markdown report following CHEERS; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Patient Cohort Study
description: Analyze a patient cohort with multi-variable outcomes
prompt: Analyze the uploaded patient cohort data (n={sample_size} patients). This is observational, so address confounding and missing data explicitly; if the primary outcome window or eligibility is ambiguous, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first, otherwise state assumptions. (1) Describe demographics and clinical characteristics (Table 1 with standardized differences where groups exist). (2) Analyze the primary outcome {outcome}, reporting absolute and relative effect sizes with 95% CIs. (3) Perform multivariable analysis adjusting for pre-specified confounders, checking model assumptions. (4) Run pre-specified subgroup analyses by {subgroups}. (5) Test interaction effects formally rather than comparing within-subgroup p-values, correcting for multiplicity. (6) Generate a comprehensive Table 1 and key figures. Set a random seed and record package versions. Research and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save figures as PNG, tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report following STROBE; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Clinical Systematic Review
description: PRISMA-compliant systematic review of clinical evidence
prompt: Conduct a systematic review on: {question}. If the PICO elements are ambiguous in a way that changes eligibility, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions and register the scope. (1) Define explicit PICO inclusion/exclusion criteria and pre-specify outcomes. (2) Search PubMed, Cochrane, and Embase with documented, reproducible queries and dates. (3) Screen titles/abstracts against the criteria, recording counts at each stage. (4) Extract data from included studies into a structured table. (5) Assess risk of bias (Cochrane RoB 2.0). (6) Perform a meta-analysis if appropriate, reporting pooled effect sizes with 95% CIs, heterogeneity (I-squared), and a forest plot; consider publication bias. (7) Generate a PRISMA flow diagram and evidence summary tables. Set a random seed for any resampling and record package versions. This is research synthesis and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save the PRISMA diagram and forest plots as PNG, extraction/results tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Clinical Guideline Summary
description: Summarize and compare clinical practice guidelines
prompt: Summarize clinical practice guidelines for: {condition}. If the scope (patient subgroup, care setting) is ambiguous in a way that changes recommendations, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Identify current guidelines from major societies ({societies}), recording publication year and version. (2) Compare recommendations across guidelines side by side. (3) Note the strength of evidence and grading scheme behind each recommendation. (4) Highlight areas of agreement and disagreement and likely reasons. (5) Create a quick-reference comparison table. (6) Identify gaps where evidence is lacking or guidelines conflict. Cite each source with a link or citation and flag any out-of-date guidance. This is a research summary and decision-support only, not clinical advice, and does not replace consulting the primary guidelines or clinical judgement. Save the comparison table as CSV and a concise Markdown summary; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Quality Improvement Analysis
description: Analyze healthcare quality metrics and improvement initiatives
prompt: Analyze healthcare quality data for: {initiative}. If the metric definition, denominator, or intervention timing is ambiguous in a way that changes conclusions, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Define quality metrics, operational numerators/denominators, and benchmarks. (2) Create statistical process control charts to assess process stability, choosing the chart type appropriate to the data. (3) Identify special-cause variation using standard run rules. (4) Perform a pre/post intervention comparison with effect sizes and 95% confidence intervals, mindful of secular trends and confounding. (5) Calculate process capability indices where appropriate. (6) Recommend next PDSA steps for sustained improvement. Follow the IHI Model for Improvement; set a random seed for any resampling and record package versions. This is research and operational decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save control charts and figures as PNG, metric tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

placeholders.label: QI initiative or metric to analyze

name: Health Equity Analysis
description: Analyze health disparities across demographic groups
prompt: Analyze health equity in the uploaded data. Outcome: {outcome}. If the comparison groups or outcome definition are ambiguous in a way that changes conclusions, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions and handle small-cell privacy. (1) Stratify outcomes by {demographics}, reporting group sizes. (2) Calculate absolute disparities (rate/risk differences) and relative disparities (ratios), each with 95% confidence intervals, plus a summary index where relevant. (3) Apply decomposition methods (e.g. Oaxaca-Blinder) to identify drivers. (4) Adjust for socioeconomic confounders while being explicit about which differences are inequities versus case-mix. (5) Map geographic variations if data permit. (6) Recommend targeted, evidence-informed interventions. Set a random seed and record package versions; address missing data. This is research and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save figures/maps as PNG, disparity tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report following STROBE; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: EHR Data Analysis
description: Analyze structured electronic health record data
prompt: Analyze the uploaded EHR dataset. EHR data is messy and prone to bias, so document data-quality decisions; if the cohort window or {analysis_goal} is ambiguous in a way that changes results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first, otherwise state assumptions. (1) Clean and harmonize clinical codes (ICD, CPT, LOINC) and standardize units. (2) Define the study cohort using inclusion criteria: {criteria}, recording attrition at each step. (3) Extract clinical features (diagnoses, labs, medications, vitals). (4) Handle temporal data with explicit index dates and time windows, avoiding look-ahead leakage. (5) Analyze: {analysis_goal}, reporting effect sizes or performance with 95% CIs and correcting for multiple comparisons. (6) Address missingness (informative missingness, imputation) and biases (ascertainment, immortal-time, indication). Set a random seed and record package versions (pyhealth, polars). Research and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save figures as PNG, cohort/result tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Longitudinal Data Analysis
description: Analyze repeated measures and panel data over time
prompt: Analyze the uploaded longitudinal data. Outcome: {outcome}, measured at {timepoints} timepoints. If the outcome scale, time coding, or comparison is ambiguous in a way that changes results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Visualize individual trajectories and group means over time. (2) Fit mixed-effects models with random intercepts and slopes, reporting fixed-effect estimates with 95% confidence intervals. (3) Test group x time interactions for {groups}, correcting for multiple comparisons where several contrasts are made. (4) Handle missing data and dropout under a clearly stated mechanism (MAR with multiple imputation, or a sensitivity analysis for MNAR). (5) Check model assumptions (residuals, normality of random effects, covariance structure). (6) Report trajectory plots and model estimates. Set a random seed and record package versions (statsmodels). Research and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save trajectory plots as PNG, estimate tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Population Health Assessment
description: Assess health status and needs of a population
prompt: Conduct a population health assessment for: {population}. If the geographic boundary, time period, or priority outcomes are ambiguous in a way that changes conclusions, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions and cite data sources with years. (1) Compile a demographic and socioeconomic profile. (2) Analyze leading causes of morbidity and mortality, reporting rates with 95% confidence intervals and noting age-adjustment. (3) Assess healthcare access and utilization. (4) Identify health risk factors and social determinants. (5) Compare with state/national benchmarks, distinguishing meaningful from chance differences and respecting small-cell suppression. (6) Prioritize health needs and recommend evidence-informed interventions. Record data vintages and any package versions used. This is a research assessment and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save figures/maps as PNG, indicator tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations and data gaps.

name: Case-Control Analysis
description: Analyze matched or unmatched case-control study data
prompt: Analyze the uploaded case-control study data. Cases: {cases}, Controls: {controls}. Confirm whether the design is matched or unmatched and how controls were sampled; if this or the exposure definition is ambiguous in a way that changes results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first, otherwise state assumptions. (1) Compare baseline characteristics of cases and controls. (2) Calculate crude and adjusted odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals. (3) Perform conditional logistic regression for matched designs (unconditional otherwise). (4) Check for confounding and effect modification (stratified/interaction analysis). (5) Assess dose-response if applicable (trend test). (6) Report with forest plots and summary tables, noting selection and recall bias. Set a random seed and record package versions (statsmodels). Research and decision-support only, not clinical advice. Save the forest plot as PNG, result tables as CSV, and a concise Markdown report following STROBE; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: EEG Signal Analysis
description: Process and analyze electroencephalography data
prompt: Analyze the uploaded EEG data. (1) Preprocess: bandpass filter ({freq_range} Hz, default 0.1-100), notch-filter line noise, re-reference (e.g., average or mastoid), mark/interpolate bad channels, and reject artifacts via ICA (eye/muscle/cardiac) with the removed components reported. (2) Compute power spectral density for standard bands (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma). (3) Epoch around and analyze event-related potentials (ERPs) for {events}, with baseline correction. (4) Compute topographic maps. (5) Perform time-frequency analysis (e.g., Morlet wavelets) with baseline normalization. (6) Run statistical comparisons between conditions using cluster-based permutation tests (or appropriate correction) to control multiple comparisons, reporting effect sizes; avoid circular selection of channels/time windows. Set a random seed and record package versions. If the event coding, montage, or reference is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures (PNG), spectral/ERP/statistics tables (CSV), the cleaned epochs object, and a methods+findings report (Markdown); summarize in chat, name files, and note limitations.

name: fMRI Analysis Pipeline
description: Process and analyze functional MRI brain imaging data
prompt: Analyze the uploaded fMRI data. (1) Preprocessing: motion correction (report and optionally censor high-motion volumes/FD), slice timing, coregistration and spatial normalization to MNI, smoothing ({fwhm} mm FWHM, default 6). (2) First-level GLM for task contrasts: {contrasts}, including motion regressors and appropriate HRF/high-pass filtering. (3) Group-level analysis (one-sample or two-sample t-test) matched to the design. (4) Cluster-level thresholding (p < 0.001 voxel, p < 0.05 cluster, FWE/FDR-corrected) to control multiple comparisons; avoid double-dipping in ROI definition. (5) Report activated regions with MNI coordinates, cluster sizes, and effect sizes. (6) Visualize activations on brain templates. Set a random seed where stochastic and record package/template versions. If the contrasts, group design, or expected effects are ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures (PNG), activation/coordinate tables (CSV), statistical maps (NIfTI), and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations including motion and multiple-comparison concerns.

name: Spike Sorting
description: Sort neural spikes from extracellular recordings
prompt: Perform spike sorting on the uploaded electrophysiology data ({recording_type}). (1) Bandpass filter (300-3000 Hz) for the spike band, apply common-reference where appropriate, and assess/remove bad channels. (2) Detect spikes using threshold-based methods (report the threshold relative to noise estimate). (3) Extract waveform features (PCA) for clustering. (4) Cluster spikes into putative single units using template-matching/density methods suited to this probe geometry. (5) Assess unit quality (ISI violations, SNR, isolation distance, presence ratio) and label units as single vs multi-unit using explicit criteria. (6) Visualize sorted units with average waveforms, autocorrelograms, and amplitude-over-time to flag drift. Set a random seed and record package versions. If the sampling rate, probe geometry, or quality thresholds are ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures (PNG), unit metrics and spike-time tables (CSV), the sorted output object, and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); then summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Neural Decoding
description: Decode stimulus or behavior from neural activity
prompt: Build a neural decoder to predict {variable} from the uploaded neural data. (1) Preprocess neural data (binning, smoothing, z-scoring) and document feature construction. (2) Split into training/test sets preserving temporal structure (blocked/contiguous folds, no leakage across time); hold out a test set untouched. (3) Train decoders (logistic regression, SVM, LSTM) with hyperparameters tuned on validation folds only. (4) Evaluate with appropriate cross-validation and report accuracy plus a chance/shuffle baseline and confidence intervals; use balanced metrics for imbalanced classes. (5) Analyze which neurons contribute most to decoding (weights/permutation importance), avoiding overinterpretation. (6) Generate confusion matrices and decoding accuracy over time. Set a random seed and record package versions. If the variable definition, trial structure, or alignment is ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures (PNG), performance and importance tables (CSV), the trained model/weights, and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); then summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

placeholders.label: Variable to decode (stimulus, choice, position, movement)

name: Brain Connectivity Analysis
description: Analyze functional and structural brain connectivity
prompt: Perform brain connectivity analysis on the uploaded data ({modality}). (1) Parcellate the brain using {atlas} (default Schaefer if unspecified), after modality-appropriate preprocessing (e.g., motion/nuisance regression and band-pass for resting fMRI; ICA cleaning for EEG; tractography QC for DTI). (2) Compute the connectivity matrix (functional: correlation/coherence, structural: streamline counts). (3) Threshold and binarize the network with a justified, consistent density across subjects. (4) Calculate graph metrics (degree, clustering, modularity, efficiency), comparing against null/random networks. (5) Identify hub regions. (6) Compare connectivity between {groups} using appropriate tests with multiple-comparison correction (e.g., NBS/FDR) and effect sizes; flag motion and edge-density confounds. Set a random seed and record package versions. If the atlas, edge definition, or groups are ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Visualize the connectome and save figures (PNG), connectivity matrices and metric tables (CSV), and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); summarize in chat, name files, and note limitations.

name: Behavioral Data Analysis
description: Analyze behavioral experiments: reaction times, choices, learning
prompt: Analyze the uploaded behavioral data from a {task_type} task. (1) QC trials (remove timeouts/outlier RTs with a stated rule) and compute summary statistics per condition (accuracy, RT). (2) Fit reaction time distributions (ex-Gaussian, drift diffusion) appropriate to the task. (3) Analyze learning curves if applicable. (4) Fit computational models: {models}, with parameter recovery checks and sensible priors/bounds. (5) Compare models using AIC/BIC (and cross-validation where feasible), reporting uncertainty and using hierarchical/mixed-effects modeling to respect subject-level grouping. (6) Correlate model parameters with {neural_data} if available, correcting for multiple comparisons and reporting effect sizes. Set a random seed and record package versions. If the task structure, model specification, or grouping is ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures (PNG), per-subject statistics and fitted-parameter tables (CSV), and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); then summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Calcium Imaging Analysis
description: Process and analyze calcium imaging recordings
prompt: Analyze the uploaded calcium imaging data. (1) Motion correction and registration, with QC of residual motion. (2) ROI segmentation (CNMF/CNMF-E or Suite2p approach) and neuropil correction, filtering low-quality ROIs. (3) Extract fluorescence traces, compute dF/F, and deconvolve to estimate spike rates (noting deconvolution is an estimate). (4) Identify place cells, time cells, or task-modulated neurons for {task} using shuffle/permutation tests with multiple-comparison correction; avoid circular selection. (5) Compute tuning curves and selectivity indices with confidence intervals. (6) Analyze population dynamics with dimensionality reduction (PCA/demixed PCA). Set a random seed and record package versions. If the task timing/alignment or cell-selection criteria are ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures (PNG), trace/tuning/cell-classification tables (CSV), the processed traces object, and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); then summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Neuropixels Data Processing
description: Process high-density Neuropixels probe recordings
prompt: Process the uploaded Neuropixels recording. (1) Apply preprocessing (common average/median reference, bandpass filter, bad-channel detection, and drift handling). (2) Run Kilosort (or equivalent) spike sorting with recorded parameters. (3) Curate sorted units (merge, split, remove noise) using objective criteria. (4) Compute unit quality metrics (ISI violations, presence ratio, amplitude cutoff, SNR) and classify single vs multi-units by explicit thresholds. (5) Extract good single units and multi-units. (6) Align spikes to behavioral events and compute PSTHs for {events} with baseline windows and appropriate smoothing; report responsiveness with statistics corrected for multiple comparisons. Set a random seed and record package versions. If the sync/alignment between ephys and behavior, or curation thresholds, are ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures including waveforms and PSTHs (PNG), unit-metric and spike tables (CSV), the curated sorting output, and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); then summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Brain Atlas Mapping
description: Map neural data onto standardized brain atlases
prompt: Map the uploaded neural recording locations or imaging data onto the {atlas} brain atlas. (1) Register electrode positions or imaging coordinates to atlas space, documenting the registration method and QC of alignment. (2) Identify the brain region for each recording site, flagging sites near region boundaries as uncertain. (3) Summarize data by brain region with counts and dispersion. (4) Create publication-quality brain maps showing: {data_type}. (5) Generate tables of region assignments with confidence/region hierarchy. (6) Visualize on 2D flatmaps or 3D renderings. Where group comparisons are made, use appropriate tests with multiple-comparison correction and report effect sizes. Record atlas/package versions for reproducibility. If the coordinate system, atlas version, or registration landmarks are ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures including brain maps (PNG), region-assignment and summary tables (CSV), and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); then summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations including registration error.

placeholders.label: Data to map (cell counts, activity, connectivity)

name: Computational Neuron Model
description: Build and simulate biophysical neuron models
prompt: Build a computational model of a {neuron_type} neuron. (1) Define the morphology (single- or multi-compartment) and justify the level of detail. (2) Set ion channel conductances based on {species} data, citing sources and recording parameter values. (3) Simulate responses to current injection ({protocol}), checking numerical stability (time step, integration method). (4) Characterize firing properties (threshold, F-I curve, adaptation, input resistance). (5) Analyze the contribution of each conductance via systematic blockade/sensitivity analysis. (6) Compare with experimental electrophysiology data if available, reporting goodness-of-fit. Set a random seed for any stochastic elements and record simulator/package versions for reproducibility. If the neuron type, species, or stimulation protocol is missing/ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions (e.g., default step-current protocol and canonical conductances). Save figures including voltage traces and F-I curves (PNG), simulation-output and parameter tables (CSV), the model definition, and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); then summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Psychophysics Analysis
description: Fit psychometric functions and analyze perceptual data
prompt: Analyze the uploaded psychophysics data from a {task} task. (1) QC trials and fit psychometric functions (cumulative Gaussian, Weibull) to {conditions}, using maximum-likelihood with lapse-rate estimation and per-subject fits. (2) Extract thresholds and slopes with confidence intervals. (3) Compare thresholds across conditions using bootstrap (and hierarchical/mixed-effects modeling to respect subject grouping), correcting for multiple comparisons and reporting effect sizes. (4) Analyze response bias (criterion in SDT). (5) Compute d-prime and ROC if a detection task. (6) Generate publication-quality psychometric curves with confidence intervals. Set a random seed and record package versions. If the task type, condition coding, or stimulus levels are ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures (PNG), per-subject threshold/slope/bias tables (CSV), and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); then summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations including small-sample and lapse considerations.

name: Compare Neural & Artificial Networks
description: Compare biological neural representations with deep network layers
prompt: Compare neural representations with deep network representations. (1) Extract neural population activity for {stimuli}, with QC and trial averaging/noise-ceiling estimation. (2) Extract activations from {model} at multiple layers using a fixed, documented preprocessing pipeline. (3) Compute representational similarity matrices (RSMs) for both, choosing a justified distance metric. (4) Compare RSMs using RSA (Spearman correlation), with permutation/bootstrap tests, multiple-comparison correction across layers, and comparison against the neural noise ceiling. (5) Identify which layer best matches the neural data, reporting confidence intervals rather than a single point. (6) Analyze what stimulus features drive the match/mismatch. Set a random seed and record model and package versions for reproducibility. If the stimulus set, model variant/checkpoint, or brain region is ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. Save figures including RSMs and layer-correlation plots (PNG), similarity and statistics tables (CSV), and a concise methods+findings report (Markdown); then summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

placeholders.label: Deep network model (ResNet, CLIP, GPT, etc.)

name: Species Distribution Model
description: Predict habitat suitability and range for species
prompt: Build a species distribution model for: {species}. If the taxon, region, or time window is ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Compile occurrence records from GBIF (record the download DOI/snapshot date) or uploaded data, clean coordinates, and correct sampling bias (spatial thinning or a target-group background). (2) Extract environmental predictors (WorldClim/CHELSA bioclim, land cover, elevation) in a stated CRS; check predictor collinearity (VIF). (3) Fit a MaxEnt or random-forest SDM with spatially blocked cross-validation to limit spatial autocorrelation. (4) Evaluate honestly (AUC, TSS, Boyce index) with uncertainty across folds. (5) Generate a habitat-suitability map. (6) Project under future climate scenarios ({scenario}) if requested, masking extrapolation beyond training space. Set a random seed; record package and dataset versions. Save the map and projections as PNG, suitability values and metrics as CSV, and a findings report as Markdown; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations and confounds.

placeholders.label: Climate scenario for projection (optional, e.g., SSP245, SSP585)

name: Biodiversity Assessment
description: Analyze species richness, diversity indices, and community composition
prompt: Perform a biodiversity assessment on the uploaded community data. First confirm the data are counts vs. relative abundances and the sampling design; if ambiguous and consequential, ask one quick question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (1) Calculate species richness and rarefaction/extrapolation curves to control for uneven sampling effort. (2) Compute diversity indices (Shannon, Simpson, Fisher's alpha) with bootstrap confidence intervals. (3) Analyze species abundance distributions. (4) Compare communities between {sites} using beta diversity (Bray-Curtis, Jaccard), noting whether turnover or nestedness dominates. (5) Ordinate (NMDS with stress reported, PCoA). (6) Test for significant differences with PERMANOVA, and check group dispersion (PERMDISP) so location effects are not confounded by heterogeneity. Set a seed and record package versions. Save ordination and diversity plots as PNG, index and test tables as CSV, and a findings report as Markdown; summarize in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Population Dynamics Modeling
description: Model population growth, predator-prey, or competition
prompt: Model population dynamics for: {system}. If the system, time horizon, or scenario is ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one quick question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Define the model structure ({model_type}) and its assumptions. (2) Estimate parameters from the uploaded data with reported uncertainty (confidence intervals or posterior credible intervals). (3) Simulate population trajectories, propagating parameter uncertainty (e.g., ensemble runs). (4) Analyze equilibria and local/global stability. (5) Perform sensitivity and (where relevant) elasticity analysis on key parameters. (6) Generate phase portraits and time-series plots. {scenario} Validate fit against held-out or observed dynamics, set a random seed, and record package versions. Save phase portraits and time series as PNG, parameter and simulation tables as CSV, and a findings report as Markdown; summarize in chat, name the files, and note assumptions and limitations.

placeholders.label: Ecological system (single species, predator-prey, competition, SIR)

name: Climate Data Analysis
description: Analyze temperature, precipitation, and climate trends
prompt: Analyze climate data for: {region}. If the region or period is ambiguous and would materially change results, ask one quick question via the interview tool first; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Acquire reanalysis/station data (e.g., ERA5, GHCN, Berkeley Earth) recording the dataset version and baseline period, and compute temperature and precipitation trends over {period} using methods robust to autocorrelation (e.g., Mann-Kendall with Sen's slope) and report confidence intervals. prompt: Analyze the uploaded social network data. If directedness, edge weighting, or node identity is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Compute network statistics (size, density, diameter, average path length, clustering coefficient), specifying directed vs undirected; (2) Calculate centrality measures (degree, betweenness, closeness, eigenvector); (3) Detect communities (Louvain), reporting modularity and stability across runs; (4) Identify bridges and brokers (e.g., high betweenness, structural holes); (5) Analyze information-flow patterns; (6) Visualize with community coloring and centrality-based node sizing. Compare key metrics against a null model (configuration/random graph) to gauge significance. Set a random seed for stochastic algorithms and record package versions. Save figures as PNG, node/edge metric tables as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize key findings in chat, name the files, and note boundary-specification and sampling limitations.

name: Social Media Sentiment Analysis
description: Analyze public sentiment from social media data
prompt: Analyze sentiment in the uploaded social media data about: {topic}. If platform, language, or the date field is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Classify posts as positive/negative/neutral using a validated NLP model, reporting accuracy/F1 against any labeled subset; (2) Track sentiment over time with confidence bands; (3) Identify key themes driving positive and negative sentiment; (4) Break down sentiment by demographic or geographic group if available, with effect sizes and CIs; (5) Detect bot or inauthentic activity and assess its impact; (6) Generate a sentiment report with actionable, caveated insights. Stress that the sample is not a representative population; avoid causal claims. Set a random seed and record package/model versions. Save figures as PNG, classified-data and summary tables as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize key results in chat, name the files, and note sampling and model limitations.

name: Policy Impact Evaluation
description: Evaluate the causal impact of a policy intervention
prompt: Evaluate the causal impact of {policy} on {outcome} using {method}. If treatment timing, the comparison group, or the running/instrument variable is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions; cite real data sources and do not fabricate. (1) Define treatment and control groups and the identifying assumptions for the chosen method; (2) Apply it with appropriate, clustered standard errors; (3) Test the key identifying assumption (parallel trends for DiD, continuity/bandwidth for RDD, balance for PSM, instrument relevance/exclusion for IV); (4) Estimate the treatment effect with 95% confidence intervals and effect size; (5) Run robustness checks (placebo/falsification tests, alternative specifications, sensitivity to bandwidth/matching); (6) Interpret findings with careful causal language and discuss policy implications. Set a seed and record package versions. Save figures as PNG, estimate tables as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize key results in chat, name the files, and note threats to validity.

name: Education Outcomes Analysis
description: Analyze student performance and educational interventions
prompt: Analyze education outcomes from the uploaded data. If outcome definition, grouping, or available covariates are ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Describe student demographics and performance distributions, flagging missingness; (2) Compare results between {groups} with effect sizes, 95% CIs, and multiple-comparison correction; (3) Analyze achievement gaps by subgroup; (4) Build a predictive model for {outcome} with proper train/test splits and cross-validation, reporting calibrated performance and key predictors while distinguishing association from causation; (5) Evaluate the effect of {intervention} if intervention data are available, controlling for SES and prior achievement and accounting for clustering within schools/classes; (6) Summarize practical implications. Set a random seed and record package versions. Save figures as PNG, result tables as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize key results in chat, name the files, and note confounding and generalizability limitations.

name: Public Opinion Analysis
description: Analyze polling data and public opinion trends
prompt: Analyze public opinion on {topic} from the uploaded polling data. If the weighting targets, question wording, or survey mode are ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions; cite real comparison polls and do not fabricate figures. (1) Weight responses to known population margins for representativeness, documenting the scheme; (2) Calculate point estimates with design-based margins of error; (3) Analyze trends over time, distinguishing real change from sampling noise; (4) Break down by demographics (age, gender, education, party, region) with CIs and multiple-comparison correction; (5) Identify opinion drivers via weighted regression, using careful (non-causal) language; (6) Compare with historical polls and flag significant shifts versus house/mode effects. Set a seed and record package versions. Save figures as PNG, estimate tables as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize key results in chat, name the files, and note coverage and nonresponse limitations.

name: Migration Analysis
description: Analyze migration patterns, flows, and determinants
prompt: Analyze migration patterns for {region}. If the spatial unit, time window, or flow definition is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions; cite real public sources (e.g., UN DESA, national statistics) and do not fabricate. (1) Map origin-destination flows from the uploaded or public data, noting definitions and coverage; (2) Quantify net migration rates per 1,000 with consistent denominators; (3) Analyze push and pull factors; (4) Model determinants with a gravity model (log-linear/Poisson PPML), reporting coefficients with 95% CIs and goodness of fit; (5) Assess demographic impact on sending and receiving regions; (6) Visualize flows on maps and chord diagrams. Use careful causal language and check robustness to specification. Set a seed and record package versions. Save figures as PNG, flow and model tables as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize key results in chat, name the files, and note data and measurement limitations.

name: Inequality Measurement
description: Measure and analyze economic or social inequality
prompt: Measure inequality in {dimension} using the uploaded data. If the income/wealth concept, equivalence scale, or weighting is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions; cite real benchmarks and do not fabricate. (1) Calculate the Gini coefficient (with bootstrap CIs) and plot the Lorenz curve, applying sample weights; (2) Compute percentile ratios (90/10, 80/20) and top/bottom shares; (3) Decompose inequality by {subgroups} (within vs between); (4) Analyze trends over time, adjusting for inflation/currency where relevant; (5) Compare with international benchmarks; (6) Identify key drivers using decomposition methods (Blinder-Oaxaca, Theil), reporting contributions with uncertainty. Set a seed for bootstrap/resampling and record package versions. Save figures as PNG, index and decomposition tables as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize key results in chat, name the files, and note definition-sensitivity and top-coding limitations.

name: Behavioral Experiment Analysis
description: Analyze data from psychology or behavioral economics experiments
prompt: Analyze data from the uploaded behavioral experiment ({design}). If the dependent variable, condition coding, or repeated-measures structure is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Check randomization balance across conditions; (2) Analyze manipulation checks; (3) Test main hypotheses with {analysis_type}, verifying its assumptions (normality, sphericity, homogeneity) and using robust alternatives if violated; (4) Analyze moderators and mediators with appropriate models; (5) Report effect sizes (Cohen's d, partial eta-squared) with 95% CIs and correct for multiple comparisons; (6) Check for demand, order, and attrition effects; (7) Produce APA-formatted results with publication-quality figures. Note observed power or sensitivity given n; use careful causal language. Set a random seed and record package versions. Save figures as PNG, result tables as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize key results in chat, name the files, and note limitations.

name: Content Analysis
description: Systematic coding and analysis of media or documents
prompt: Perform content analysis on the uploaded documents/media. If the unit of analysis, category definitions, or language is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Develop an explicit codebook for {categories} with definitions and decision rules; (2) Apply automated coding via NLP and validate against a hand-coded subset; (3) Calculate inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa, or Krippendorff's alpha for multiple coders) and report agreement; (4) Analyze code frequency and co-occurrence with effect sizes; (5) Compare content across {groups} or time periods using appropriate tests with multiple-comparison correction; (6) Identify dominant frames and narratives, distinguishing prevalence from interpretation. Set a random seed and record package/model versions. Save figures as PNG, coded-data and frequency tables as CSV, and a Markdown findings report to the sandbox; summarize key findings in chat, name the files, and note coding-validity and corpus limitations.

name: Solve Differential Equations
description: Analytical and numerical solutions to ODEs and PDEs
prompt: Solve the differential equation(s): {equation}. (1) Classify the equation (ODE/PDE, order, linearity, homogeneity) and check dimensional consistency; state assumptions. If the domain or any parameter is ambiguous and would change the solution, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Attempt an analytical/closed-form solution (sympy) where possible, or a series/asymptotic solution otherwise. (3) Implement a numerical solution with an appropriate method (stiff vs nonstiff solver, adequate spatial discretization for PDEs) and report tolerances; perform a step-size or mesh-refinement convergence check. (4) Apply boundary/initial conditions: {conditions}. (5) Analyze solution stability and qualitative behavior; verify the numerical solution against the analytic case where available. (6) Visualize the solution and phase portraits. Set the seed, record sympy/scipy versions. Save plots as PNG, solution data as CSV, and a Markdown derivation; name the files and note validity limits.

name: Solve an Optimization Problem
description: Find optimal solutions for constrained or unconstrained problems
prompt: Solve the optimization problem: minimize/maximize {objective} subject to constraints: {constraints}. (1) Classify the problem (LP, QP, MILP, convex/nonconvex nonlinear, multi-objective) and state assumptions and units; check that the feasible region is nonempty. If the objective sense or any bound is ambiguous and would change the result, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Select an appropriate solver and justify it (convexity, differentiability, integrality). (3) Find the optimal solution and verify optimality conditions (KKT / duality gap) or use multiple random starts for nonconvex problems. (4) Perform sensitivity analysis on key parameters (shadow prices where applicable). (5) Analyze the Pareto front if multi-objective. (6) Visualize the feasible region and optimum. Set the seed, record pymoo/scipy versions. Save plots as PNG, the solution and sensitivities as CSV, and a Markdown summary; name the files and note validity limits.

name: Bayesian Inference
description: Build Bayesian models and compute posteriors
prompt: Perform Bayesian inference for: {model}. Prior assumptions: {priors}. (1) Specify the generative model and likelihood with units; justify priors and run a prior predictive check. If the data, likelihood family, or priors are ambiguous and would materially change posteriors, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Define prior distributions explicitly. (3) Run MCMC sampling (NUTS) with sufficient chains and warmup. (4) Check convergence: trace plots, R-hat < 1.01, adequate ESS, and no divergences. (5) Report posterior distributions with 95% HDIs and posterior means/medians. (6) Compare candidate models using WAIC/LOO. (7) Run posterior predictive checks and validate against known parameters on simulated data where possible. Set the seed, record pymc/arviz versions. Save trace and posterior plots as PNG, posterior summaries as CSV, and a Markdown write-up; name the files and note validity limits.

name: Monte Carlo Simulation
description: Estimate quantities through stochastic sampling
prompt: Design and run a Monte Carlo simulation for: {problem}. (1) Define the random variables and their distributions with units and any correlations; state assumptions. If the distributions or the target quantity are ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Specify the model/function to evaluate, and where possible a deterministic or analytic check of a limiting case. (3) Draw {n_samples} samples with a fixed seed. (4) Estimate the quantity of interest with a standard error and confidence interval. (5) Analyze the convergence rate (error scaling as 1/sqrt(N)) and report whether N is sufficient. (6) Apply variance reduction if needed (importance sampling, antithetic or control variates) and quantify the efficiency gain. Visualize the distribution of results. Record numpy/scipy versions. Save plots as PNG, samples/estimates as CSV, and a Markdown summary; name the files and note validity limits.

name: Graph Theory Analysis
description: Analyze graph properties, algorithms, and optimization
prompt: Analyze the uploaded graph/network. (1) Load the graph, state whether it is directed/weighted, and compute basic properties (|V|, |E|, density, diameter, degree distribution). If the file format or edge semantics are ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Run the requested graph algorithms: {algorithms}, reporting complexity and results. (3) Detect communities/connected components and report modularity. (4) Calculate centrality measures (degree, betweenness, eigenvector, PageRank). (5) Test structural properties (planarity, bipartiteness, connectivity). (6) Solve any specified optimization problem on the graph and verify against a known small case. Set the seed, record networkx versions. Save the network visualization (with an appropriate layout) and metric plots as PNG, node/edge metrics as CSV, and a Markdown summary; name the files and note validity limits.

placeholders.label: Algorithms to run (shortest path, MST, max flow, coloring)

name: Symbolic Computation
description: Exact algebraic manipulation, simplification, and proofs
prompt: Perform symbolic computation for: {expression}. (1) Parse and simplify the expression, stating the domain and any assumptions on variables (real, positive, etc.). If the variables or constraints are ambiguous and would change the result, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Perform the requested operations: {operations}, exactly with sympy. (3) Verify results numerically at sample points (and against known identities or limiting cases) to catch errors. (4) Provide a clear step-by-step derivation. (5) Generate LaTeX output of the key results. (6) Plot symbolic functions if they represent curves or surfaces. Record sympy version. Save plots as PNG, results as CSV where tabular, and the derivation as Markdown (with embedded LaTeX); name the files and note any domain restrictions or validity limits.

placeholders.label: Operations (differentiate, integrate, series expand, solve, factor)

name: Numerical Methods Implementation
description: Implement and compare numerical algorithms
prompt: Implement numerical methods for: {problem}. (1) State the problem precisely with units and the exact or reference solution used for error measurement; note assumptions. If the problem or accuracy target is ambiguous and would change the comparison, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Implement the {methods} algorithms with clean, documented code. (3) Analyze the empirical convergence rate/order and compare with theory. (4) Compare accuracy vs computational cost (error vs work/time). (5) Analyze numerical stability and error propagation (conditioning, round-off). (6) Test on benchmark problems with known answers and visualize convergence and error plots (log-log). Set the seed, record numpy/scipy versions. Save plots as PNG, error/timing tables as CSV, and a Markdown summary; name the files and note validity limits.

placeholders.label: Numerical problem (root finding, integration, linear algebra, ODE)

name: Stochastic Process Modeling
description: Model and simulate random processes
prompt: Model the stochastic process: {process}. (1) Define the state space, parameters (with units), and transition rules; state assumptions. If parameters or the time horizon are ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Derive key properties analytically where possible (mean, variance, stationary/limiting distribution, autocorrelation). (3) Simulate sample paths with an appropriate scheme (e.g. Euler-Maruyama for SDEs) and check step-size sensitivity. (4) Estimate parameters from data if provided, with confidence intervals. (5) Compute hitting times and absorption probabilities, validating simulated values against the analytic results. (6) Visualize sample paths and probability distributions. Set the seed, record numpy/scipy versions. Save plots as PNG, simulated statistics as CSV, and a Markdown write-up; name the files and note validity limits.

placeholders.label: Stochastic process (random walk, Markov chain, Poisson, Brownian motion, SDE)

name: Game Theory Analysis
description: Analyze strategic interactions and find equilibria
prompt: Analyze the game: {game}. (1) Model it as a normal-form or extensive-form game, listing players, strategy sets, and payoffs with assumptions (rationality, information). If players, strategies, or payoffs are ambiguous and would change the equilibria, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Find all pure-strategy Nash equilibria. (3) Find mixed-strategy Nash equilibria and verify the indifference conditions. (4) Identify and iteratively eliminate dominated strategies. (5) Compute Shapley values if it is a coalitional game. (6) Simulate repeated-play dynamics (e.g. best-response or replicator) and report convergence. Interpret the equilibria in context, noting efficiency and any equilibrium-selection issues. Set the seed, record package versions. Save payoff-matrix and dynamics plots as PNG, equilibria as CSV, and a Markdown summary; name the files and note validity limits.

name: Topological Data Analysis
description: Apply persistent homology and TDA to data
prompt: Apply topological data analysis to the uploaded dataset. (1) Inspect the data, choose a metric, and normalize/scale as appropriate; state assumptions. If the intended grouping or distance metric is ambiguous and would change results, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Build a filtration (Vietoris-Rips or alpha complex), justifying the maximum scale. (3) Compute persistent homology (H0, H1, H2). (4) Generate persistence diagrams and barcodes, and distinguish significant features from noise (e.g. via bootstrap confidence bands). (5) Compare persistence landscapes between groups if applicable, with a permutation test. (6) Interpret the topological features (loops, voids, components) in the data context. Set the seed, record package versions. Save persistence diagrams and barcode plots as PNG, feature/persistence data as CSV, and a Markdown summary; name the files and note validity limits.

name: Fourier Analysis
description: Frequency decomposition and spectral analysis
prompt: Perform Fourier analysis on the uploaded signal/data. (1) Inspect the sampling rate and length; state assumptions and check the Nyquist limit. If the sampling rate or units are missing and would change interpretation, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Compute the FFT and power spectrum with correct frequency-axis scaling. (3) Identify dominant frequencies and their amplitudes, with a noise-floor or significance threshold. (4) Apply windowing (Hann, Hamming) to reduce spectral leakage and note the resolution/leakage trade-off. (5) Generate a spectrogram for time-frequency analysis. (6) Filter the signal: {filter_spec}, reporting the filter design and its effect; compare original and filtered signals (and verify via Parseval/energy). Report all significant frequency components with units. Record numpy/scipy versions. Save spectra and spectrogram plots as PNG, frequency components as CSV, and a Markdown summary; name the files and note validity limits.

name: Dynamical Systems Analysis
description: Analyze stability, bifurcations, and chaos in dynamical systems
prompt: Analyze the dynamical system: {system}. (1) State variables, parameters, and units; check dimensional consistency and any assumptions. If parameter values or the domain are ambiguous and would change behavior, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool, else state assumptions. (2) Find all equilibrium points analytically where possible. (3) Linearize and classify stability via Jacobian eigenvalues (node, saddle, focus, center). (4) Generate phase portraits with representative trajectories and nullclines. (5) Identify bifurcations as {parameter} varies (saddle-node, Hopf, etc.). (6) Construct bifurcation diagrams. (7) Compute Lyapunov exponents to detect chaos, checking convergence of the estimate, and visualize attractors and basins of attraction. Set the seed, record numpy/scipy/sympy versions. Save phase portraits, bifurcation, and attractor plots as PNG, numerical data as CSV, and a Markdown write-up; name the files and note validity limits.

name: Build a Classifier
description: Train classification models with evaluation and interpretation
prompt: Build a classification model to predict {target} from the uploaded data. If the positive class or priority metric is ambiguous and would change conclusions, ask one clarifying question via the interview tool; otherwise state assumptions. (1) Run EDA and quantify class balance; screen for leakage. (2) Make a stratified train/validation/test split, with preprocessing and feature engineering fit inside the pipeline; handle imbalance (class weights or resampling) on training only. (3) Establish a baseline, then train multiple models (logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, SVM), tuning on validation/CV. (4) Evaluate on held-out test data: accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC-ROC, PR-AUC, confusion matrix, and calibration. (5) Interpret with SHAP. (6) Report final tuned performance with uncertainty. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save the model, predictions CSV, figures PNG, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: NLP Text Analysis
description: Analyze text with classification, NER, summarization, or embedding
prompt: Perform NLP analysis on the uploaded text data. Task: {task}. (1) Inspect the corpus: size, language, label balance, duplicates, and text-length distribution; preprocess (clean, normalize encoding, tokenize) without leaking test text. (2) Make a train/validation/test split (stratified for classification), establish a simple baseline (e.g., TF-IDF + linear model), then apply {task} via a fine-tuned or pretrained transformer, tuning on validation only. (3) Evaluate on the held-out test set with task-appropriate metrics (F1/accuracy, entity-level F1, ROUGE, or retrieval metrics) and uncertainty. (4) Analyze errors and edge cases by slice. (5) Visualize results (confusion matrix, attention/saliency, or embedding clusters). (6) Export predictions. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save predictions CSV, figures PNG, the model artifact, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

placeholders.label: NLP task (classification, NER, sentiment, summarization, embedding)

name: Image Classification
description: Build image classifiers with CNNs and transfer learning
prompt: Build an image classifier for: {classes} using the uploaded image dataset. (1) Explore the dataset: per-class counts, image sizes, duplicates, and label quality; address class imbalance. (2) Make a stratified train/validation/test split with no leakage across augmented copies, then apply augmentation to training only. (3) Use transfer learning from {backbone} (choose a sensible default if unspecified), fine-tuning with an appropriate learning-rate schedule and early stopping on validation. (4) Evaluate on the held-out test set: accuracy, per-class precision/recall/F1, confusion matrix, and calibration. (5) Visualize Grad-CAM attention maps and inspect failure cases. (6) Report final performance with uncertainty. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save the trained weights, predictions CSV, figures PNG, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Anomaly Detection
description: Detect outliers and anomalies in datasets
prompt: Build an anomaly detection system for the uploaded data. (1) Inspect schema, missingness, and scales; visualize the data distribution and any known labels. (2) Preprocess and scale features, then apply multiple methods (Isolation Forest, Local Outlier Factor, Autoencoder, DBSCAN). (3) Compare flagged anomalies across methods (agreement, overlap) and combine into a consensus score. (4) Characterize anomalous points by their distinguishing features. (5) Tune detection thresholds to satisfy {constraint} (expected anomaly rate or false-positive tolerance); if labels exist, report precision/recall and PR-AUC. (6) Report per-record anomaly scores and flagged records. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save a scored-records CSV, figures PNG, the detector artifact, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

placeholders.label: Constraint (expected anomaly rate, false positive tolerance)

name: Feature Engineering
description: Create informative features from raw data
prompt: Perform feature engineering on the uploaded dataset for predicting {target}. (1) Analyze existing features: distributions, correlations, mutual information with the target, and missingness; screen for leakage and target-derived features. (2) Create interaction, polynomial, aggregation, and domain-specific features. (3) Handle categorical variables with appropriate encoding (one-hot, target/ordinal) fit inside cross-validation to prevent leakage. (4) Apply dimensionality reduction if high-dimensional. (5) Select top features using multiple methods (LASSO, random-forest importance, Boruta) and assess stability across folds. (6) Validate that engineered features improve a simple model on held-out data over a baseline. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save the engineered dataset and a feature-importance table as CSV, figures PNG, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize top features in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Hyperparameter Tuning
description: Optimize model hyperparameters with systematic search
prompt: Optimize hyperparameters for the {model} model on the uploaded data. (1) Inspect data, set a held-out test set, and define a justified search space for key hyperparameters. (2) Run {method} (default Bayesian/Optuna if unspecified) with {n_trials} trials (default 100), evaluating via {n_folds}-fold cross-validation (default 5) with preprocessing inside the CV pipeline to prevent leakage. (3) Establish the default-config baseline for comparison. (4) Analyze hyperparameter importance and interactions. (5) Report the optimal configuration and its cross-validated performance with uncertainty, then confirm on the untouched test set to check for overfitting to validation. (6) Visualize the optimization history and landscape. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save the trials table as CSV, the best model, figures PNG, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

placeholders.label: Model type (XGBoost, random forest, SVM, neural network)

name: Model Interpretability
description: Explain ML model predictions with SHAP, LIME, and feature analysis
prompt: Interpret the uploaded trained model's predictions. First confirm the model type, feature schema, and the data used for explanation. (1) Compute global SHAP values for all features and report ranked importance. (2) Generate SHAP summary, dependence, and force plots. (3) Apply LIME to {n_samples} individual predictions (default 10), spanning correct and incorrect cases. (4) Create partial dependence and ICE plots for top features. (5) Analyze feature interactions and check stability of explanations across samples. (6) Identify prediction patterns, spurious correlations, and potential biases, cross-checking SHAP against permutation importance. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save interpretability figures as PNG, a feature-attribution table as CSV, and a Markdown report of key insights to the sandbox; summarize findings in chat, name files, and state limitations.

placeholders.label: Number of samples for local explanations (default: 10)

name: Transfer Learning
description: Adapt pretrained models to new tasks and domains
prompt: Apply transfer learning for: {task} using {source_model}. (1) Load the pretrained model, confirm input/preprocessing compatibility, and analyze layer representations. (2) Make a train/validation/test split for the target task and screen for leakage. (3) Freeze appropriate layers and add a task-specific head for {task}. (4) Fine-tune with learning-rate warmup and decay, early stopping on validation, comparing feature-extraction vs full fine-tuning. (5) Evaluate on the target test set with task-appropriate metrics and uncertainty, benchmarking against training from scratch and a simple baseline. (6) Analyze where transfer helps and where it hurts (e.g., by data size or class). Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save the adapted model, predictions CSV, learning-curve PNGs, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Graph Neural Network
description: Build GNNs for node, edge, or graph-level prediction
prompt: Build a graph neural network for: {task} on the uploaded graph data. (1) Construct the graph (nodes, edges, features) and report basic statistics (degree, components, homophily); check for leakage in splits. (2) Use task-appropriate splits (transductive masks for nodes, edge splits for link prediction, graph-level splits otherwise). (3) Establish a non-graph baseline (e.g., MLP on node features), then select and train a GNN (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, GIN) with an appropriate loss, tuning on validation only. (4) Evaluate on the held-out test set: {metrics} (use sensible defaults if unspecified) with uncertainty. (5) Analyze learned node embeddings. (6) Visualize attention weights or message passing, and compare against the baseline. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save the model, predictions/embeddings CSV, figures PNG, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.

placeholders.label: Task (node classification, link prediction, graph classification)

name: Build a Generative Model
description: Train VAE, GAN, or diffusion model for data generation
prompt: Build a generative model to generate: {data_type}. (1) Analyze the training-data distribution and preprocessing; reserve a held-out set for evaluation. (2) Choose architecture: {architecture} (pick a sensible default for the data type if unspecified) and justify it. (3) Train with an appropriate loss and regularization, monitoring for mode collapse or posterior collapse and using early stopping. (4) Evaluate generation quality with suitable metrics (FID/IS for images, validity/novelty/uniqueness for molecules, perplexity for text, or distributional fidelity for tabular) plus a held-out comparison. (5) Generate {n_samples} samples (default 100). (6) Analyze latent-space interpolation and mode coverage versus the real distribution. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save generated samples, the model artifact, figures PNG, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize quality in chat, name files, and state limitations.

placeholders.label: Data type to generate (images, molecules, text, tabular)

name: Reinforcement Learning
description: Train RL agents for sequential decision-making
prompt: Train an RL agent for: {environment}. (1) Define the state space, action space, reward function, and episode termination; note any reward-shaping assumptions. (2) Select algorithm: {algorithm} (choose a sensible default for the action space if unspecified) and justify it. (3) Train with documented hyperparameters across multiple random seeds to capture variance. (4) Plot learning curves (episode reward and length) with mean and confidence band across seeds. (5) Evaluate the trained policy over many deterministic episodes versus a random baseline, reporting mean return with uncertainty. (6) Visualize the agent's behavior and decision-making. Set and report the random seeds and key package versions. Deliverables: save the trained policy, an evaluation-metrics CSV, learning-curve PNGs, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize results in chat, name files, and state limitations.

name: Model Comparison & Selection
description: Systematically compare ML models and select the best
prompt: Compare ML models for predicting {target} from the uploaded data. Models to compare: {models}. (1) Inspect data, class balance, and leakage, then fix one identical preprocessing pipeline and train/test split (or nested CV) applied to every model. (2) Establish a trivial baseline, then train all models with the same cross-validation folds, tuning each fairly on validation only. (3) Compare on multiple task-appropriate metrics (accuracy, AUC, F1, RMSE, calibration) with per-fold uncertainty. (4) Test whether differences are statistically significant (paired t-test or Friedman with post-hoc), reporting effect sizes. (5) Analyze computational cost (train/inference time, memory). (6) Generate a comprehensive comparison table and recommend the best model with rationale. Set and report a random seed and key package versions. Deliverables: save the comparison table as CSV, figures PNG, and a Markdown report to the sandbox; summarize in chat, name files, and state limitations.
